CITY OF SAN MATEO
City Council Regular Meeting
June 5, 2023
7:00 PM
City Hall Council Chamber
Entrance is on O’Farrell Street
COUNCIL MEMBERS
Amourence Lee, Mayor
Lisa Diaz Nash, Deputy Mayor
Rich Hedges
Adam Loraine
Rob Newsom Jr.
 
AMENDED AGENDA
THIS MEETING CAN BE ATTENDED IN PERSON OR REMOTE BY JOINING ZOOM – SEE CODES BELOW.
PUBLIC COMMENTS WILL BE ACCEPTED BOTH IN PERSON AND REMOTELY.
SEE END OF AGENDA FOR OPTIONS AND INSTRUCTIONS ON HOW TO PARTICIPATE IN THE MEETING. 
To join via Zoom – click here: June 5, 2023
To join via telephone:  (408) 638-0968      
Webinar ID: 843 0477 0493      Passcode: 374106
The City Council meeting will conclude by 11:00 p.m. unless otherwise extended by council vote. 
Any unheard items will automatically move forward to the next regular meeting. 
Amendment: Item Number 26 was moved from New Business to Consent Calendar.

All matters listed under the Consent Calendar are considered by the Council to be routine and will be enacted by one motion without
discussion. If discussion is desired, that item may be removed and considered separately.
 
3.
City Council Meeting Minutes - Approval
Approve the minutes of the special and regular meetings of May 15, 2023.
CEQA: This minutes approval is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative
activity that will not result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section
15378(b)(5).)

originally scheduled for October 2, 2023.

not result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)

5.
Senior Transportation "Get Around" Program – Agreement
Approve an agreement with Serra Yellow Cab of Daly City Inc. for the Senior Transportation “Get Around”
program up to the amount of $150,000 for fiscal year 2023-24, with an option for the City to extend the
agreement for fiscal year 2024-2025 for up to $150,000 for a potential contract total of $300,000; and authorize
the Director of Parks and Recreation to execute the agreement in substantially the form presented.
CEQA: This is not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that it will not cause a physical
change in the environment. (Public Resources Code Section 21065.)
6.
Summer Camp Bus Transportation – Agreement
Approve an agreement with First Student, Inc. to provide bus transportation services for City of San Mateo
summer camps from June 12, 2023 through August 11, 2023 for a contract amount of $118,272; establish a
contingency reserve of 10%; and authorize the Director of Parks and Recreation to execute the agreement in
substantially the form presented.
CEQA: This is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity that will not
result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)
7.
Fiscal Year 2023-24 General Obligation Bond – Tax Rate Levy
Adopt a Resolution to levy the property tax rate of $0.0057 per $100 of assessed value, equating to $5.70 per
$100,000 assessed value), for the voter-approved General Obligation Bond annual debt service payment.
CEQA: This GO Bond Tax Rate Levy is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is a government fiscal activity
which does not involve any commitment to any specific project which may result in a potentially significant
physical impact on the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)
8.
Wastewater Treatment Plant Supply of Sodium Bisulfite – Change Order
Approve Contract Change Order No. 1 with Univar Solutions USA, Inc., to increase the supply of sodium bisulfite
by 20,000 gallons, for an amount not to exceed $32,340, for a total agreement amount of $355,734; and
authorize the Public Works Director to execute the change order in substantially the form presented.
CEQA: This Contract Change Order No. 1 is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is a government fiscal activity
which does not involve any commitment to any specific project which may result in a potentially significant
physical impact on the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)
9.
Wastewater Treatment Plant Supply of Sodium Hypochlorite – Change Order
Approve Contract Change Order No. 1 with Univar USA, Inc., to increase the supply of sodium hypochlorite by
20,000 gallons for an amount not to exceed $32,704, for a total agreement amount of $523,254; and authorize
the Public Works Director to execute the change order in substantially the form presented.
CEQA: This Contract Change Order #1 is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is a government fiscal activity
which does not involve any commitment to any specific project which may result in a potentially significant
physical impact on the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)
10.
Wastewater Treatment Plant Supply of Chemicals – Contracts
Award chemical supply contracts to: 1) Hasa, Inc. to supply sodium hypochlorite in the amount of $957,027; 2)
Univar Solutions USA, Inc. to supply sodium bisulfite in the amount of $404,517; and 3) Kemira Water Solutions,
Inc. for the supply of ferric chloride in the amount of $312,212; establish 10% contingency reserves for each
contract; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the contracts in substantially the form presented

and issue change orders within the contingency amounts.
CEQA: This contract is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is a government fiscal activity which does not
involve any commitment to any specific project which may result in a potentially significant physical impact on the

11.
Wastewater Treatment Plant Supply of Liquid Emulsion Polymer – Contract
Adopt a Resolution to approve an alternative purchasing procedure to award a contract to SNF Polydyne for the
supply of liquid emulsion polymer in the amount of $405,613; establish a 10% contingency reserve; and authorize
the Public Works Director to execute the contract in substantially the form presented and issue change orders
within the contingency amount.
CEQA: This contract is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is a government fiscal activity which does not
involve any commitment to any specific project which may result in a potentially significant physical impact on the
environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)
12.
Wastewater Treatment Plant Immediate Action Projects, Package III – Amendment
Approve Amendment No. 5 to the professional services agreement with HDR Engineering, Inc. to provide
engineering services during construction for the Wastewater Treatment Plant Immediate Action Projects Package
III Project and increase the budget by $422,000, for a total amount not to exceed $1,521,495; increase the
contingency reserve by an amount of $42,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the
amendment in substantially the form presented and issue change orders within the contingency amount.
CEQA: The City prepared a Final Programmatic Environmental Impact Report (Final PEIR) in accordance with the
California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) (SCH No. 2015032006). Council on June 6, 2016, certified the PEIR. In
accordance with CEQA, the Final PEIR serves as the primary environmental compliance document for the
implementation of the Clean Water Program. The City prepared Addenda to the Final PEIR that was approved on
July 16, 2018 (Addendum 1) and April 15, 2019 (Addendum 2). This work is within the scope of the Project
evaluated in the PEIR and Addendum.
13.
Hillsdale Caltrain Station Bicycle Access Gap Closure Project – Amendment
Adopt a Resolution to authorize an appropriation in the amount of $93,948 of Measure A funds to the Hillsdale
Caltrain Station Bicycle Access Gap Closure project; amend the agreement with Bellecci & Associates, Inc. for the
Hillsdale Caltrain Station Bicycle Access Gap Closure project to increase the agreement amount by $93,948 for a
new total not to exceed $246,948; increase the contingency amount by $88,255 for new contingency total of
$98,255; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the amendment in substantially the form presented
and issue change orders within the contingency amount.
CEQA: This amendment is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity
that will not result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)
(5).)/span>
14.

CEQA: This action to adopt a resolution is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or
administrative activity that will not result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA
Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)

15.
On-Call Professional Consultant Services – Amendments
Approve Amendment No. 2 to extend the term of individual on-call professional consultant services agreements
for no additional cost with Wilsey Ham, HDR Engineering, Inc., and Bellecci & Associates until December 31,
2025; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute future no-cost amendments.
CEQA: This approval of no-cost agreements extensions is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an
organizational or administrative activity that will not result in direct or indirect physical changes in the
environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)
16.
Citywide Street Reconstruction Phase 4 – Agreement and Budget Transfer
Adopt a Resolution to authorize a budget transfer from Citywide Street Reconstruction Package 1 to Citywide
Street Reconstruction Package 3 in the amount of $410,000; affirm a requested bid relief from Ghilotti Bros, Inc.;
approve an agreement with McGuire and Hester for the Citywide Street Reconstruction Phase 4 Project in the
amount of $6,259,358; establish a contingency reserve in the amount of $630,000; and authorize the Public
Works Director to execute the agreement in substantially the form presented and issue change orders within the
contingency amount.
CEQA: This project is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the operation,
repair, maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures,
facilities, mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of existing or
former use. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15301.)
17.
Citywide Street Rehabilitation Package 5B – Agreement
Approve an agreement with MNS Engineers, Inc. for design services in an amount not to exceed $585,000;
establish a contingency reserve of $58,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the agreement in
substantially the form presented and approve amendments within the contingency amount.
CEQA: This project is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the operation,
repair, maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures,
facilities, mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of existing or
former use. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15301.)
18.
Bermuda Drive Bridge Replacement Project Pacific Gas & Electric – Grant of Easement
Adopt a Resolution to grant an Easement Deed to Pacific Gas & Electric and authorize the City Manager to
execute the Easement Deed.
CEQA: The proposed activity is exempt from CEQA, because it involves granting an easement for the installation of
a new small structure, namely an underground PG&E vault. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15303.)

authorize the City Manager to execute the amendment in substantially the form presented.
CEQA: This action of approving Amendment No. 4 is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational
or administrative activity that will not result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA
Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)

Resources Director to execute the agreement in substantially the form presented.
CEQA: This project is exempt from CEQA requirements in that it can be seen with certainty that there is no
possibility that the activity in question may have a significant effect on the environment per Guidelines Section
15061(b)(3).

PUBLIC COMMENT
Members of the public wishing to comment on any item not appearing on the agenda may address the City Council at this time. State
law prevents Council from taking action on any matter not on the agenda; your comments may be referred to staff for follow up.
Public comment is limited to a total of 15 minutes; however, an opportunity for additional public comment may be provided later in
the agenda.
 
PUBLIC HEARING
City Council action on the following public hearing(s) represents a final decision concluding all administrative proceedings. Judicial
review may be had only if a petition is filed with the Court not later than the 90th day following the date the decision is made.
Judicial review of environmental determinations may be subject to a time period for litigation as short as 30 days following the date
the decision is made.
 
29.
Sewer Rate Increase for Fiscal Years 2023-24 to 2027-28 – Ordinance Introduction
Conduct a public hearing, and if there is a determination that a majority protest does not exist, introduce an
ordinance to establish maximum sewer rates effective August 1, 2023 through June 30, 2028 pursuant to San
Mateo Municipal Code Section 3.54.030 “Sewer Service Charges.”
CEQA: In accordance with CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4), the adoption of this ordinance is not a project
subject to CEQA, because the sewer service charge is a funding mechanism for the City Sewer operations and
capital projects.

ADJOURNMENT

AGENDAS: Agendas and material are posted on the City’s website on the Friday preceding each Council Meeting and can be viewed on the City's
website at www.cityofsanmateo.org . Any supplemental material distributed to the Council after the posting of the agenda will be made part of the
official record.
WATCHING A MEETING ON TV: City Council meetings are broadcast live on Comcast/channel 27, Wave/channel 26, or AT&T/channel 99.
For transmission problems during the broadcast, please call (650) 522-7099. For all other broadcast comments, call (650) 522-7040, Monday-Friday,
8 a.m. - 5 p.m.
WATCHING A MEETING ON A COMPUTER: There are three ways to stream.
1) Public Meeting Portal www.cityofsanmateo.org/publicmeetings
2) City YouTube channel and stream it on YouTube: http://youtube.com/CityofSanMateo
3) Watch TV live stream: https://www.cityofsanmateo.org/193/Channel-San-Mateo-Live-Stream 
PUBLIC COMMENTS/REQUEST TO SPEAK
Prior to the Meeting 
Send comments to: clerk@cityofsanmateo.org until 4 p.m. the day of the meeting.
During the meeting
By Zoom: Click the link at the top of the agenda and you’ll be added to the meeting. All attendees are muted by default. When the item of interest
is open for consideration, select the “Raise Your Hand” icon and you will be called on at the appropriate time.
By telephone: Call (408) 638-0968 and enter the conference ID found at the top of the meeting agenda. When the item of interest is open for

--- page 8 ---
consideration, select *9 to raise your hand. When called upon, press *6 to unmute, state your name and provide your comments.
In Person: At the meeting complete a “Request to Speak” form, submit a request at the speaker kiosk or scan the QR code.
ACCESSIBILITY: In compliance with the Americans with Disabilities Act, those with disabilities requiring special accommodations to participate in
this meeting may contact the City Clerk’s Office at (650) 522-7040 or clerk@cityofsanmateo.org . Notification 48 hours prior to the meeting will
enable the City to make reasonable arrangements to ensure accessibility to this meeting.

City Council Special Meeting Minutes – 5/15/2023
Page 1
COUNCIL MEMBERS
Amourence Lee, Mayor
Lisa Diaz Nash, Deputy Mayor
Rich Hedges
Adam Loraine
Rob Newsom Jr. 
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Special Meeting Minutes
City Council
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
 
May 15, 2023
City Hall Council Chambers 5:30 PM
Special Meeting
 
CALL TO ORDER at 5:30 p.m. 
Roll Call
Present:
Deputy Mayor Diaz Nash, Council Members: Loraine and Newsom
Absent:
Mayor Lee
STUDY SESSION
1.
4th and Railroad Parking Lot – Bespoke Development Term Sheet
Kathy Kleinbaum, Assistant City Manager, provided a presentation on the proposed Term Sheet for the public-
private partnership Bespoke development project, which includes the City-owned parking lot at 4th and Railroad 
Avenue. Developers Carlos Castellanos, Alta Housing, Vice President, Real Estate Development, and Preston 
O’Connell, Harvest Properties, Partner, said a few words about their companies and answered questions.
Council asked questions of staff.
Public Comment - Linda Lam, Self-Help for the Elderly Volunteer, requested that Council allow space in the new 
building for Self-Help; Thomas Morgan suggested using the loan could be used as equity for the entire project and 
incentivize the developers to work more quickly on the housing.
Council by consensus confirmed the current financial terms and supported the project.
2.
Downtown – Public Restroom
Evan Albert, Deputy Director of Public Works, provided a presentation to consider and outline the options for the 
opening of a public restroom in Downtown.
Public Comment – Lew Cohen, Downtown San Mateo, stated it is a public right, basic human right, to offer a public 
bathroom and it should be in a central location. Erica Wood, President and CEO of Chamber of Commerce, stated 
public restrooms support local businesses too, allows seniors and families to spend more time downtown.
Council discussion ensued. Consensus was in support of opening a public restroom at the transit center and then 
to do a study session in one year’s time to consider other alternatives or ideas and include input from CalTrain.
ADJOURNMENT –The meeting adjourned at 6:46 p.m.
APPROVED BY:                                                        
SUBMITTED BY:
Amourence Lee, Mayor                      
Patrice Olds, City Clerk

8.
Wastewater Treatment Plant Immediate Action Projects, Package III – Appropriation and Construction 
Agreement
Adopt a Resolution appropriating $8,574,000 from Fund 72 Reserves to the Wastewater Treatment 
Plant (WWTP) Immediate Action Projects, Package III (IAP III) project; award an agreement to Mountain 
Cascade, Inc. for construction of the IAP III Project in an amount not to exceed $11,344,696; establish a 
contingency fund in an amount not to exceed $2,270,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to 
execute the agreement in substantially the form presented and issue change orders within the 
contingency amount.
Enactment:  Resolution No. 47 (2023)

Renewable Diesel Fuel Procurement and Delivery Services – Amendment
Approve Amendment No. 4 to exercise a third, one-year extension to the agreement with Bosco Oil, Inc. 
dba Valley Oil Company for procurement and delivery of red and clear renewable diesel, and increase 
the amount of the extension from $150,000 to $300,000 for a new agreement total of $1,125,000; 
establish a contingency reserve in the amount of $75,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to 
execute the agreement in substantially the form presented and issue change orders within the 
contingency amount.

Gasoline Fuel Procurement and Delivery Services – Amendment
Approve Amendment No. 4 to exercise a third, one-year extension to the agreement with Pinnacle 
Petroleum, Inc. for gasoline fuel procurement and delivery, and increase the amount of the extension 
from $250,000 to $500,000 for a new total agreement of $1,875,000; establish a contingency reserve in 
the amount of $100,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the agreement in 
substantially the form presented and issue change orders within the contingency amount.

Contractors, Inc. for a term of two years, in an amount not to exceed $500,000 per year, with up to two,

authorize the Public Works Director to execute the agreement in substantially the form presented and 
execute any amendments within the agreement amount.

OLD BUSINESS
14.
Pedestrian Mall – Boundary Determination
Kathy Kleinbaum, Assistant City Manager, provided a presentation on whether to bring forward a revision to the 
boundaries of the pedestrian mall in order to reopen for traffic the block of B Street between First and Second 
Avenues and whether to allow for parklet platforms within the pedestrian mall. Council asked questions of staff.
Public Comment – Kevin Simpson stated concern that other temporary measures could be used to make the mall 
function better, noting it takes time, persistence and imagination. Jonathan New stated not to make policy 
decisions on restaurants that may not be here that long, make the decisions based on residents who live here. 
David Hunt stated keep the streets closed to cars. Hamdi Uger, Porterhouse, expressed there is no attractive 
atmosphere, and a vision is needed to make upscale street for pedestrian use. Seema Patel commented on the 
businesses on 1st and 2nd avenues and stated lack of infrastructure is the problem equaling lack of vibrancy. 
Tanner Walters advocated to keep the pedestrian mall on both streets and keep the conversation going because 
businesses seem split. Tyson Mao, Wursthall Restaurant, spoke of the difficulty by no longer having outdoor 
dining. City and local businesses should collaborate and he supports the pedestrian mall idea. Rick Ballard advises 
to keep B Street closed and to plan for the future, not the past. Sarah Fields stated a desire to keep the section of 
B Street closed for pedestrians. Sean Lacson stated he wants to keep streets closed to cars. Michael stated B Street 
is not a pedestrian mall; it is a blocked off street and nothing more it needs to go upscale. Pam Rittelmeyer voiced 
that it was safer without cars on the street and it is nice and vibrant. Kailun Wu remarked keeping B Street closed 
offers for a better quality of life. Jordan Grimes said the streets are for human beings and noted people’s habits 
have changed since the pandemic with less people in offices so need to shift to new models to attract to area. 
Erica Wood emphasized the need to do everything possible to have vibrant community spaces; working on this 
now is timely, and downtown revitalization is a priority; create experiences that draw people downtown.
Following additional questions to staff, Council provided comments and reached consensus to continue to keep 
both blocks closed; proceed to the next stage of improvements for those blocks; and to allow for parklets on both 
of the blocks. 
Council recessed from 8:57 p.m. to 9:10 p.m.
15
Update and Direction - Flood Relief Fund
Drew Corbett, City Manager, provided an update on the Winter Storm Damage Relief effort and noted the 
remaining funds in the designated fund. Council asked questions of staff. There was no public comment. Council 
approved, by consensus, staff’s recommendation to return the remainder of funds to the General Fund.
NEW BUSINESS
16.
Climate Action Plan – Annual Progress Report
Andrea Chow, Sustainability Analyst, provided a progress report on the implementation of the Climate Action Plan. 
Council asked questions of staff.
Public Comment – Robert Whitehair, SMCAT, expressed congratulations for the workshop held last Tuesday and 
encouraged the City to take more bold action. David Hunt highly recommends efficient e-bikes; and supports 
infrastructure improvements to make this more doable. Mike Swire, SMCAT Citizen’s Advisory Committee, would 
like to see specificity addressing the need to handle transportation and reduce traffic. Michael Weinhauer stated 
the need to reduce greenhouse gas emissions, but that more electric infrastructure is needed.
Council acknowledged the City’s great work that has already been done and noted that there is still more to do. 
Council asked questions regarding a pending court ruling involving the City of Berkeley lawsuit pertaining to the 
banning of new natural gas infrastructure in new developments and requested an update when that is known.

PUBLIC HEARING
18.
100 N Humboldt St. – Property Owner Appeal of Permanent Relocation
Christina Horrisberger, Director of Community Development Department, provided a presentation with Kristin Aiu, 
Code Enforcement Officer, and Linh Nguyen, Assistant City Attorney, assisted with questions. Staff reviewed the 
history of issues with the property, outlined the process and steps taken as per the municipal code. Council asked 
questions of staff.
The Deputy Mayor opened the Public Hearing and invited the Appellant’s Presentation. Attorney Gina Papan spoke 
on behalf of Owner Treyana Pierce. The tenants did not present.
Public Comment – Treyana Pierce asked why did HUD approve the house two years in a row if it was in such poor 
condition. Kiera Aldridge read a letter, commented on placing of tenants’ refrigerators. Marta Zavaler, Tenant, 
refuted the landlord’s statements, the tenants did not cause the issues and noted this was not malicious process 
to get money. Mellie stated she had issues with the tenants living there and supports Treyana. Rick Sakuda stated 
code enforcement came to a conclusion without gathering all the facts and supports Treyana. Angelina Soldatos, 
California Apartment Association, encouraged a process with extreme caution and scrutiny, and the need for a 
clear procedure. Jordan Grimes, One San Mateo, stated this is the condition this ordinance was meant to address. 
Diane Papan stated the eviction was prior to the relocation assessment and opined that the City should not do 
relocation costs prior to communicating with the owner and stated this is not the code the prior council adopted.
Landlord rebuttal – Ms. Papan stated there was no communication with the owner but remediation after the fact 
and noted the mitigation was working. There is no law that requires an owner to inspect, the tenants did not 
notify the owner and the process was not clearly established.
The Deputy Mayor closed the Public Hearing. Council provided comments and discussion continued with 
additional questions of staff on process, due process and if the landlord should have been notified prior to the 
issuing of the relocation payment. Council provided comments on the process that was used. The City Manager 
stated staff followed the correct process as outlined in the City’s Municipal Code. 
Motion to grant the property owner’s appeal contesting the notice to reimburse the City of San Mateo for the 
permanent relocation benefits to the tenants who reside at 100 N. Humboldt St. pursuant to San Mateo Municipal 
Code Chapter 7.50 “Property Owner Obligations with Respect to Tenants Displaced from Unsafe or Substandard 
Units” and direct staff to bring back a written statement of decision at the following meeting. Motion passed 3-1.
Moved:
Hedges, Seconded: Diaz Nash

TO:

FROM:
Drew Corbett, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
City Clerk's Office
MEETING DATE:
June 05, 2023 
SUBJECT:
City Council 2023 Regular Meeting Calendar Update – Adoption
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt the updated City Council regular meeting schedule to re-establish the regular City Council meeting originally 
scheduled for October 2, 2023.
BACKGROUND:
On April 3, 2023, the City Council adopted an updated City Council regular meeting schedule in preparation for the City’s 
delegation to Japan to celebrate the 60th anniversary of the Sister City relationship between Toyonaka and San Mateo. The 
travel for this event is scheduled between October 1, 2023, and October 9, 2023. The City Clerk’s Office had, prior to the 
April 3, 2023, City Council meeting, received confirmation of interest from Mayor Lee, Deputy Mayor Diaz Nash, and 
Council Member Loraine. Given the three Council Member interest, the regular meeting schedule was updated to cancel 
the October 2, 2023, due to a lack of quorum.   
Since this action was taken, the City Clerk’s Office received confirmation from Deputy Mayor Diaz Nash and Council 
Member Loraine that they were no longer able to participate in the delegation to Toyonaka. While Mayor Lee will continue 
to participate in the delegation, the City Council will be able to make quorum on October 2, 2023 and can re-establish this 
as a regular meeting date. 
Staff plans to bring to City Council three meetings in the month of October on the draft General Plan, including a special 
meeting, to be scheduled at a later date. Re-establishing the October 2, 2023 regular meeting will facilitate this business. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
There is no budget impact associated with this administrative action
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This item is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity that will not result in 
direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.
ATTACHMENTS
Att 1 - Proposed Updated Calendar 2023 Tracked Changes

CITY OF SAN MATEO
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
Agenda Number:  5
Section Name: CONSENT CALENDAR
Account Number: 11-6114
File ID: 23-7424
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Drew Corbett, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Parks and Recreation Department
MEETING DATE:
June 05, 2023 
SUBJECT:
Senior Transportation "Get Around" Program – Agreement
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve an agreement with Serra Yellow Cab of Daly City Inc. for the Senior Transportation “Get Around” program up to 
the amount of $150,000 for fiscal year 2023-24, with an option for the City to extend the agreement for fiscal year 2024-
2025 for up to $150,000 for a potential contract total of $300,000; and authorize the Director of Parks and Recreation to 
execute the agreement in substantially the form presented.
BACKGROUND:
The Senior Transportation “Get Around” program has been facilitated by the Parks and Recreation Department since 
January 1, 2018. Currently there are 1,061 San Mateo seniors signed up for the program. From January 1, 2022-December 
31, 2022, 4,055 one-way rides were provided. On average the “Get Around” program has between 120-130 active riders a 
month. The current transportation vendor, Serra Yellow Cab of Daly City Inc., provides on-demand transportation services 
for qualified City of San Mateo residents aged 60 and over. Each month a rider is eligible for eight one-way trips or four 
round-trips to destinations within the cities of San Mateo, Burlingame, Foster City, Belmont, Hillsborough, San Carlos and 
Redwood City. Additional locations outside of the cities listed include the Veterans' Administration Medical Campus in 
Menlo Park and Palo Alto and Stanford University Medical Centers. All rides must begin or end in the City of San Mateo. 
 
Serra Yellow Cab of Daly City Inc. was selected as the current transportation vendor as they are equipped to service the 
local senior population. Their fleet consists of over 150 cabs, of which approximately 15% are wheelchair and scooter 
accessible. They provide overflow transportation services to the SamTrans "Ready Wheels" program, which requires their 
staff to be trained to handle the needs of passengers with disabilities. All Serra Yellow Cab of Daly City Inc. vehicles are 
equipped with "Cab Connect" tracking system that can accurately track the start and end of travel locations, distance 
traveled and the necessary billing information. Serra Yellow Cab of Daly City Inc. is the only cab company that has a permit 
to operate in San Mateo with the capacity to manage the current scale of the “Get Around” program. Per the City's 
purchasing ordinance (3.60.050) exceptions (b), it is possible to award an agreement without the required RFP (Request 
for Proposal) procedure if it is deemed impracticable, unavailing, or impossible to call for bids through a competitive 
process. Staff has determined that no other transportation companies that are licensed by the City of San Mateo have the 
necessary fleet size to meet the requirements of this contact.
BUDGET IMPACT:
$150,000 has been budgeted in the 2024 budget cycle and this agenda report is for contract authority only.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This is not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that it will not cause a physical change in the 
environment. (Public Resources Code Section 21065.)

CITY OF SAN MATEO
RESOLUTION NO.
(2023)
APPROVING ALTERNATIVE PURCHASING PROCEDURE FOR AN AGREEMENT WITH SERRA YELLOW CAB, 
INC. FOR “GET AROUND” SENIORS TRANSPORTATION PROGRAM
WHEREAS, the City of San Mateo “Get Around” Program (“Program”) for seniors transportation, has 
been operated by the Parks and Recreation Department since January 1, 2018; and
WHEREAS, in 2022 the Program allowed 1,061 San Mateo seniors to take a total of 4,055 low-cost rides; 
and
WHEREAS, the competitive process required by the Purchasing policy for Professional Services over 
$100,000 is impracticable as Serra Yellow Cab, Inc. is the only business that can meet the Program’s needs, with 
over 150 vehicles in operation;  other licensed operators have no more than 10 vehicles in operation; and
WHEREAS, Municipal Code Section 3.60.050(b) provides that exceptions to the City Council Award 
Procedures are possible if the Council makes a finding concurrent with the award that calling for bids for that 
particular agreement is impracticable, unavailing, or impossible; and 
WHEREAS, City staff recommends approval of the agreement with Serra Yellow Cab, Inc. for up to 
$150,000. 
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, HEREBY FINDS and 
RESOLVES that:
1. The agreement with Serra Yellow Cab, Inc. is not a project as defined by the California Environmental 
Quality Act in that it is an organizational or administrative activity that will not result in direct or indirect physical 
changes in the environment.
2. Pursuant to Municipal Code Section 3.60.050(b), calling for bids would be impracticable, unavailing, or 
impossible because Serra Yellow Cab, Inc. is the only City of San Mateo licensed business operator with enough 
cabs to meet the Program requirements.
3. The Parks and Recreation Director is authorized to execute the agreement in substantially the form 
submitted.

the services of CONTRACTOR.

Price Index escalator.

the right to terminate this Agreement or suspend work on the Project for any reason, upon ten (10) days' 
written notice to CONTRACTOR.  CONTRACTOR agrees to cease all work under this Agreement upon 
receipt of said written notice.

be the property of CITY, whether the project for which they are made is executed or not.

this Agreement are confidential until released by CITY to the public.  CONTRACTOR shall not make any 
such documents or information available to any individual or organization not employed by 
CONTRACTOR or CITY without the written consent of CITY before any such release.

SECTION 16 - COSTS AND ATTORNEY FEES 
 
 
Attorney fees in total amount not exceeding $5000, shall be recoverable as costs (by the filing 
of a cost bill) by the prevailing party in any action or actions to enforce the provisions of this 
Agreement.  The above $5000 limit is the total of attorney fees recoverable whether in the trial court, 
appellate court, or otherwise, and regardless of the number of attorneys, trials, appeals, or actions.  It 
is the intent of this provision that neither party shall have to pay the other more than $5000 for 
attorney fees arising out of an action, or actions to enforce the provisions of this Agreement. 
 
SECTION 17 - NON-DISCRIMINATION 
 
 
CONTRACTOR warrants that it is an Equal Opportunity Employer and shall comply with 
applicable regulations governing equal employment opportunity.  Neither CONTRACTOR nor any of its 
subcontractors shall discriminate in the employment of any person because of race, color, national 
origin, ancestry, physical handicap, medical condition, marital status, sex, or age, unless based upon a 
bona fide occupational qualification pursuant to the California Fair Employment and Housing Act. 
 
SECTION 18 - MEDIATION 
 
 
Should any dispute arise out of this Agreement, any party may request that it be submitted to 
mediation.  The parties shall meet in mediation within 30 days of a request.  The mediator shall be 
agreed to by the mediating parties; in the absence of an agreement, the parties shall each submit one 
name from mediators listed by either the American Arbitration Association, the State Mediation and 
Conciliation Service, or other agreed-upon service.  The mediator shall be selected by a blind draw. 
 
 
The cost of mediation shall be borne equally by the parties.  Neither party shall be deemed the 
prevailing party.  No party shall be permitted to file a legal action without first meeting in mediation 
and making a good faith attempt to reach a mediated settlement.  The mediation process, once 
commenced by a meeting with the mediator, shall last until agreement is reached by the parties but 
not more than 60 days, unless the maximum time is extended by the parties.

Contract for Services  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Page 7 of 11 
Rev 12/22 
EXHIBIT A 
 
SCOPE OF SERVICES 
 
The CITY OF SAN MATEO Senior Transportation “Get Around” Program provides limited on demand 
transportation services for City of San Mateo residents aged 60+ that have been qualified for the 
program by City of San Mateo staff.  
 
The CONTRACTOR will be notified of eligible participants on an as needed basis and prior to the rider’s  
initial call to obtain service from the contractor. The information that will be provided will include the  
name, San Mateo address, phone number of the patron, and if the patron requires a specific type of  
assisted transportation. 
 
The CONTRACTOR will use the Cab Connect software system to track all of the rides. The City of San  
Mateo will provide Senior Transportation “Get Around” program cards to the qualified participants. In 
the event of a system failure the Contractor will provide a written voucher for all manually entered 
rides, this will include the start and end points of the trip, the metered amount, and a name and 
signature of the participant who used the trip. All manually entered rides need to be clearly identified 
on the monthly invoice. 
 
Riders are eligible to take one-way trips to any destination within the cities of San Mateo, Burlingame,  
Foster City, Belmont, Hillsborough, San Carlos and Redwood City. All trips will start or end in the City of  
San Mateo. Riders can also make direct trips to the Veterans Administration Medical Campus - Menlo  
Park Division (795 Willow Road, 94025) and Veterans Administration Medical Campus -Palo Alto  
Division (3801 Miranda Avenue, 94304) and Stanford University Medical Center and their medical  
offices (300 Pasteur Drive, 94305).  
 
The CITY OF SAN MATEO will not pay for any rides that start or end outside of the specified program  
boundaries. 
 
The CITY OF SAN MATEO will retain the right to adjust the number of rides that program participants 
may take including adjusting monthly and yearly maximums. 
 
The CONTRACTOR will provide transfer assistance to passengers needing assistance in and out of the  
vehicle upon request. The transfer assistance will be limited to cab to curb and curb to cab, excludes  
any stairs, and drivers will not carry any patrons.  
 
The CONTRACTOR will provide wheelchair and scooter enabled vehicles upon request. The contractor  
will secure the patron’s wheelchair or scooter to the vehicle.  
 
CONTRACTOR will respond to on demand requests within 30 minutes of the call for standard vehicles  
and up to 45 minutes for wheelchair and scooter-equipped vehicles.  
 
CONTRACTOR will provide background and driving record checks on all employees with direct access to  
riders. 
 
28 of 763

--- page 29 ---
Contract for Services  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Page 8 of 11 
Rev 12/22

EXHIBIT B 
 
PAYMENT RATES 
 
CONTRACTOR will submit an itemized invoice to the CITY by the 20th of each month to evidence all 
rides provided. 
 
Invoices should be sent to: 
 
San Mateo Parks and Recreation 
 
Attn: Senior Transportation “Get Around” Program 
 
330 West 20th Avenue 
 
San Mateo, CA 94403 
 
The CITY will send invoiced payment within 30 days of receipt of the invoice. 
 
Payments should be sent to: 
 
Serra Yellow Cab of Daly City Inc. 
 
Attn: Talib Salamin 
 
195 87th Street #D 
 
Daly City, CA 94015 
 
Transportation rates will be charged at the following rates: 
Rides that over 5 miles will be charged $25.10 starting fee, plus $3.00 per mile on the 5 plus mile, plus 
15% service fee. Any rides that are less than five miles will be charged a five-mile rate of $25.10. 
Wheelchair accessible rides will be charged a $40.00 starting fee (flag drop), plus $3.00 dollars a mile, 
plus 15% service fee.  Any wheelchair accessible rides that are less than five miles will be charged a 
five-mile rate of $40.00. An additional $0.30 click charge will be incurred for trips slowed by excessive 
traffic or other uncontrollable circumstances. The senior patrons will pay the first $5.00 and the 
remaining fee will be paid by the City.  
  
The City of San Mateo will not pay for any gratuity or tipping. Any tips will be left to the rider’s  
discretion. 
  
All fees, taxes and services under this Agreement shall not exceed $150,000 for fiscal year 2023-2024 
and if CITY chooses option to extend an additional year, Agreement shall not exceed $150,0000 for 
fiscal year 2024-2025.  Total Agreement shall not exceed $300,000.

EXHIBIT C 
 
INSURANCE REQUIREMENTS 
 
 
MINIMUM SCOPE OF INSURANCE  
 
Coverage shall be at least as broad as: 
1. Commercial General Liability (CGL):  Insurance Services Office (ISO) Form CG 00 01 12 07 
covering CGL on an “occurrence” basis, including products-completed operations, personal & 
advertising injury, with limits no less than $2,000,000 per occurrence. If a general aggregate limit 
applies, either the general aggregate limit shall apply separately to this project/location or the 
general aggregate limit shall be twice the required occurrence limit. 
2. Automobile Liability:  ISO Form Number CA 00 01 covering any auto (Code 1), or if Contractor 
has no owned autos, hired, (Code 8) and non-owned autos (Code 9), with limit no less than 
$1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury and property damage. 
3. Workers’ Compensation: as required by the State of California, with Statutory Limits, and 
Employer’s Liability Insurance with limit of no less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury 
or disease.  
If the contractor maintains higher limits than the minimums shown above, the City requires and shall be 
entitled to coverage for the higher limits maintained by the contractor. 
Other Insurance Provisions 
The insurance policies are to contain, or be endorsed to contain, the following provisions: 
Additional Insured Status 
The City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents are to be covered as insureds on 
the auto policy for liability arising out of automobiles owned, leased, hired or borrowed by or on behalf 
of the Contractor; and on the CGL policy with respect to liability arising out of work or operations 
performed by or on behalf of the Contractor including materials, parts or equipment furnished in 
connection with such work or operations. General liability coverage can be provided in the form of an 
endorsement to the Contractor’s insurance (at least as broad as ISO Form CG 20 10, 11 85 or both CG 
20 10 and CG 20 37 forms if later revisions used). 
Primary Coverage 
For any claims related to this contract, the Contractor’s insurance coverage shall be primary insurance 
as respects the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents. Any insurance or self-
insurance maintained by the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, or agents shall be 
excess of the Contractor’s insurance and shall not contribute with it. 
Notice of Cancellation 
Each insurance policy required above shall provide that coverage shall not be canceled, except after 
thirty (30) days’ prior written notice (10 days for non-payment) has been given to the City.

Waiver of Subrogation 
Contractor hereby grants to City a waiver of any right to subrogation which any insurer of said Contractor 
may acquire against the City by virtue of the payment of any loss under such insurance.  Contractor 
agrees to obtain any endorsement that may be necessary to effect this waiver of subrogation, but this 
provision applies regardless of whether or not the City has received a waiver of subrogation 
endorsement from the insurer.   
Deductibles and Self-Insured Retentions 
Any deductibles or self-insured retentions must be declared to and approved by the City. The City may 
require the Contractor to purchase coverage with a lower deductible or retention or provide proof of 
ability to pay losses and related investigations, claim administration, and defense expenses within the 
retention.   
Acceptability of Insurers 
Insurance is to be placed with insurers with a current A.M. Best’s rating of no less than A:VII, unless 
otherwise acceptable to the City. 
Verification of Coverage 
Contractor shall furnish the City with original certificates and amendatory endorsements or copies of 
the applicable policy language effecting coverage required by this clause.  All certificates and 
endorsements are to be received and approved by the City before work commences.  However, failure 
to obtain the required documents prior to the work beginning shall not waive the Contractor’s 
obligation to provide them.  The City reserves the right to require complete, certified copies of all 
required insurance policies, including endorsements required by these specifications, at any time.

TO:
City Council
FROM:
Drew Corbett, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Parks and Recreation Department
MEETING DATE:
June 05, 2023 
SUBJECT:
Summer Camp Bus Transportation – Agreement
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve an agreement with First Student, Inc. to provide bus transportation services for City of San Mateo summer camps 
from June 12, 2023 through August 11, 2023 for a contract amount of $118,272; establish a contingency reserve of 10%; 
and authorize the Director of Parks and Recreation to execute the agreement in substantially the form presented.  
BACKGROUND:
For the 2023 summer camp session, the City will be hosting summer camps at various parks and facilities throughout San 
Mateo for participants ages 6-14. Over the 9 weeks of camps over 1,400 participants will be accommodated. A key 
component of these camps will be field trips. These field trip locations include Great America, Bel Mateo Bowl, Coyote 
Point, and Oakland Zoo, to name a few. In addition, buses are needed to transport participants to local City aquatics 
facilities for weekly swimming and to local County public parks. 
 
On February 13, 2023, a formal request for proposals (RFP) was posted with all proposals being due by 5:00pm on 
February 28, 2023. The City received a total of three responses, and First Student Inc. was selected based on their ability to 
provide the services requested in the RFP scope. The other two vendors who responded were unable to guarantee the 
necessary number of buses needed during the timeframe requested. First Student Inc. attests to having “a century of 
experience and dedicated service to showcase their commitment to safety.” First Student Inc. successfully provided bus 
services for the City of San Mateo for prior summer camps.
BUDGET IMPACT:
$118,272 plus a 10% potential contingency reserve is included in the Parks and Recreation operating budget, and this 
agenda report is for contract authority only.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity that will not result in direct 
or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

SECTION 15 - WAIVERS

The waiver by either party of any breach or violation of any term, covenant, or condition of this
Agreement or of any provisions of any ordinance or law shall not be deemed to be a waiver of such
term, covenant, condition, ordinance or law or of any subsequent breach or violation of the same or of
any other term, condition, ordinance, or law. The subsequent acceptance by either party of any fee or
other money which may become due hereunder shall not be deemed to be a waiver of any preceding
breach or violation by the other party of any term, covenant, or condition of this Agreement or of any
applicable law or ordinance.

SECTION 16 - COSTS AND ATTORNEY FEES

Attorney fees in total amount not exceeding $5000, shall be recoverable as costs (by the filing
of a cost bill) by the prevailing party in any action or actions to enforce the provisions of this
Agreement. The above $5000 limit is the total of attorney fees recoverable whether in the trial court,
appellate court, or otherwise, and regardless of the number of attorneys, trials, appeals, or actions. It
is the intent of this provision that neither party shall have to pay the other more than $5000 for
attorney fees arising out of an action, or actions to enforce the provisions of this Agreement.

SECTION 17 - NON-DISCRIMINATION

CONTRACTOR warrants that it is an Equal Opportunity Employer and shall comply with
applicable regulations governing equal employment opportunity. Neither CONTRACTOR nor any of its
subcontractors shall discriminate in the employment of any person because of race, color, national
origin, ancestry, physical handicap, medical condition, marital status, sex, or age, unless based upon a
bona fide occupational qualification pursuant to the California Fair Employment and Housing Act.

SECTION 18 - MEDIATION

Should any dispute arise out of this Agreement, any party may request that it be submitted to
mediation. The parties shall meet in mediation within 30 days of a request. The mediator shall be
agreed to by the mediating parties; in the absence of an agreement, the parties shall each submit one
name from mediators listed by either the American Arbitration Association, the State Mediation and
Conciliation Service, or other agreed-upon service. The mediator shall be selected by a blind draw.

The cost of mediation shall be borne equally by the parties. Neither party shall be deemed the
prevailing party. No party shall be permitted to file a legal action without first meeting in mediation
and making a good faith attempt to reach a mediated settlement. The mediation process, once
commenced by a meeting with the mediator, shall last until agreement is reached by the parties but
not more than 60 days, unless the maximum time is extended by the parties.

EXHIBIT A
SCOPE OF SERVICES

Response Time

CONTRACTOR will be required to have vehicles at the pick-up area(s) a minimum of

30 minutes prior to the trip. From time-to-time San Mateo may require additional bus service
for field trips and other transportation services, vehicles must be at the designated pick-up
area(s) a minimum of 30 minutes prior to the scheduled departure.

Additions/deletions of Routes

Although this agreement identifies specific routes/services required, it is hereby agreed and
understood that any route/service may be added or deleted to/from this contract at the

option of San Mateo. When an addition to this contract is required, CONTRACTOR shall submit price
quotes for these new routes/services and may be added to the contract in San Mateo’s best interest.

Programs to be Serviced by Buses and Drivers
Camp Mateo: Buses with drivers needed for nine (9) camp weeks. The camp will run from June 12,
2023 through August 11, 2023.

Week 1: June 12, 2023-June 16 ,2023: Ten (10) buses and drivers must be provided
Week 2: June 19, 2023-June 23, 2023: Ten (10) buses and drivers must be provided
Week 3: June 26, 2023-June 30, 2023: Ten (10) buses and drivers must be provided
Week 4: July 5, 2023-July 7, 2023: Six (6) buses and drivers must be provided

Week 5: July 10, 2023-July 14, 2023: Ten (10) buses and drivers must be provided
Week 6: July 17, 2023-July 20, 2023: Ten (10) buses and drivers must be provided
Week 7: July 24, 2023-July 28, 2023: Ten (10) buses and drivers must be provided
Week 8: July 31, 2023-August 3, 2023: Ten (10) buses and drivers must be provided
Wek 9: August 7, 2023-August 11, 2023: Ten (10) buses and drivers must be provided

X-treme Summer Days: Buses with drivers needed for nine (9) camp weeks. The camp will run from
June 12, 2023 through August 11, 2023.

Week 1: June 12, 2023-June 16 ,2023: Six (6) buses and drivers must be provided
Week 2: June 19, 2023-June 23, 2023: Five (5) buses and drivers must be provided
Week 3: June 26, 2023-June 30, 2023: Three (3) buses and drivers must be provided
Week 4: July 5, 2023-July 7, 2023: Two (2) buses and drivers must be provided

Week 5: July 10, 2023-July 14, 2023: Three (3) buses and drivers must be provided
Week 6: July 17, 2023-July 20, 2023: Five (5) buses and drivers must be provided
Week 7: July 24, 2023-July 28, 2023: Four (4) buses and drivers must be provided
Week 8: July 31, 2023-August 3, 2023: Four (4) buses and drivers must be provided
Week 9: August 7, 2023-August 11, 2023: Four (4) buses and drivers must be provided

Buses and Drivers shall meet the following conditions:

Contract for Services
Rev 12/22

41 of 763


--- page 42 ---
1. Buses shall be certified by the State meeting requirements to transport school children a minimum
of fifty-five (55) passengers with radio dispatch.

2. CONTRACTOR will meet all Federal and State rules, regulations, statutes, and ordinances, including
proper driver’s license, bus certification, etc.

3. Rental fee to include all gasoline charges. All maintenance of all vehicles, including any towing
charges incurred by disabled buses, will be the CONTRACTOR responsibility.

4. Any claims of damage must be reported to the Parks & Recreation Director. Any necessary repairs
for the damage must be submitted in writing with cost estimated and pre-approved by the Parks &
Recreation Director before repairs are started.

5. Number of hours and buses are approximations and specific numbers are dependent on other
factors. CONTRACTOR will be given as much advanced notice as possible, but not less than 24 hours.

6. Radio communications between the bus drivers and the bus dispatcher must be in effect and
operating at all times.

7. CITY reserves the right to cancel bus use for individual trips by giving 24 hours’ notice without
penalty. If CONTRACTOR cancels, a minimum of 24 hours’ notice will be required to the CITY. Full-
refund of cost of services cancelled shall be issued to the CITY within (4) weeks.

8. A Program Supervisor riding the bus may request changes in the bus schedule or stops due to
weather conditions or emergency situations. Driver must recognize staff requests for schedule changes
and emergency stops and honor them.

9. All prices shall include parking/toll charges. Buses must stay with the group for the duration of the
individual trip unless otherwise directed by the Program Supervisor.

10. All buses must stay with Parks & Recreation Camps and not go on another trip/location and leave
our camp children at the program location without a bus. Bus and bus driver must stay with the camp

event due to possible inclement weather or/an emergency situation.

11. Bus driver and Program Supervisor shall remain in contact during each trip. Program Supervisor will
supply bus driver with cell phone number and bus driver is expected to do the same.

EXHIBIT C

INSURANCE REQUIREMENTS

MINIMUM SCOPE OF INSURANCE

Coverage shall be at least as broad as:

1. Commercial General Liability (CGL): Insurance Services Office (ISO) Form CG 00 01 12 07
covering CGL on an “occurrence” basis, including products-completed operations, personal &
advertising injury, with limits no less than $2,000,000 per occurrence. If a general aggregate limit
applies, either the general aggregate limit shall apply separately to this project/location or the
general aggregate limit shall be twice the required occurrence limit.

2. Automobile Liability: ISO Form Number CA 00 01 covering any auto (Code 1), or if Contractor
has no owned autos, hired, (Code 8) and non-owned autos (Code 9), with limit no less than
$5,000,000 per accident for bodily injury and property damage.

3. Workers’ Compensation: as required by the State of California, with Statutory Limits, and
Employer’s Liability Insurance with limit of no less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury
or disease.

If the contractor maintains higher limits than the minimums shown above, the City requires and shall be
entitled to coverage for the higher limits maintained by the contractor.

Other Insurance Provisions
The insurance policies are to contain, or be endorsed to contain, the following provisions:

Additional Insured Status

The City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents are to be covered as insureds on
the auto policy for liability arising out of automobiles owned, leased, hired or borrowed by or on behalf
of the Contractor; and on the CGL policy with respect to liability arising out of work or operations
performed by or on behalf of the Contractor including materials, parts or equipment furnished in
connection with such work or operations. General liability coverage can be provided in the form of an
endorsement to the Contractor’s insurance (at least as broad as ISO Form CG 20 10, 11 85 or both CG
20 10 and CG 20 37 forms if later revisions used).

Primary Coverage

For any claims related to this contract, the Contractor’s insurance coverage shall be primary insurance
as respects the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents. Any insurance or self-
insurance maintained by the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, or agents shall be
excess of the Contractor’s insurance and shall not contribute with it.

Notice of Cancellation

Each insurance policy required above shall provide that coverage shall not be canceled, except after
thirty (30) days’ prior written notice (10 days for non-payment) has been given to the City.

Contract for Services
Rev 12/22

50 of 763


--- page 51 ---
Waiver of Subrogation

Contractor hereby grants to City a waiver of any right to subrogation which any insurer of said Contractor
may acquire against the City by virtue of the payment of any loss under such insurance. Contractor
agrees to obtain any endorsement that may be necessary to effect this waiver of subrogation, but this
provision applies regardless of whether or not the City has received a waiver of subrogation
endorsement from the insurer.

Deductibles and Self-insured Retentions

Any deductibles or self-insured retentions must be declared to and approved by the City. The City may
require the Contractor to purchase coverage with a lower deductible or retention or provide proof of

ability to pay losses and related investigations, claim administration, and defense expenses within the
retention.

Acceptability of Insurers

Insurance is to be placed with insurers with a current A.M. Best’s rating of no less than A:VII, unless
otherwise acceptable to the City.

Verification of Coverage

Contractor shall furnish the City with original certificates and amendatory endorsements or copies of
the applicable policy language effecting coverage required by this clause. All certificates and
endorsements are to be received and approved by the City before work commences. However, failure
to obtain the required documents prior to the work beginning shall not waive the Contractor’s
obligation to provide them. The City reserves the right to require complete, certified copies of all
required insurance policies, including endorsements required by these specifications, at any time.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
Agenda Number:  7
Section Name: CONSENT CALENDAR
Account Number: 10-2021
File ID: 23-7520
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Drew Corbett, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Finance Department
MEETING DATE:
June 05, 2023 
SUBJECT:
Fiscal Year 2023-24 General Obligation Bond – Tax Rate Levy
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to levy the property tax rate of $0.0057 per $100 of assessed value, equating to $5.70 per $100,000 
assessed value), for the voter-approved General Obligation Bond annual debt service payment.
BACKGROUND:
General Obligation Bonds (GO Bonds) are typically issued to finance government improvements benefiting the community 
as a whole. These obligations require 2/3rd voter approval and are secured by an annual tax levy on all assessed property 
within a jurisdiction. Due to the strength of this security pledge, General Obligation Bonds are typically the lowest cost 
form of public financing. 
In 1999, voters approved a $35 million bond issue to finance construction of a new main library and improvements to the 
City’s branch libraries. These bonds were issued in two series, $12 million in 2000 (GO Bonds, Series 2000) and the 
remaining $23 million in 2004 (GO Bonds, Series 2004A). The total outstanding balance of these two bonds as of June 30, 
2012 was $29.16 million. In February 2013, City Council authorized the refinancing of these two bond issues. General 
Obligation Refunding Bonds, Series 2013, were issued for $27.295 million and resulted in net present value savings of $8.4 
million over the life of the remaining outstanding bonds. Correspondingly, starting in 2014, this refinancing reduced the 
annual assessment rate paid by property owners by approximately 20%. 
An annual resolution is required to provide the County of San Mateo the authority to include this levy on the annual tax 
roll. The proposed tax levy for fiscal year 2023-24 is calculated to be $5.70 per $100,000 of assessed value, which 
represents a 5.0% reduction from the prior year levy of $6.00 per $100,000 of assessed value. The recommended 
reduction in the tax levy is due to the projected growth in the City’s assessed value from 2022-23 to 2023-24. The City’s 
total net taxable assessed valuation for fiscal year 2022-23 is $34.6 billion, compared to the prior year’s $32.0 billion. The 
levy rate for 2023-24 assumes a 4.63% increase in assessed value, bringing the total to $36.2 billion. This calculation of the 
tax levy is sufficient to meet debt service requirements as shown in Attachment 2. 
As shown in Attachment 4, the outstanding principal of the GO Bonds, Series 2013, as of the end of fiscal year 2023-24 will 
be $13.0 million, and the final debt service payment will be made in fiscal year 2030-31. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
The recommended General Obligation Bond tax rate of $0.0057 per $100 of assessed value for fiscal year 2023-24 is 
estimated to raise adequate funds, approximately $2.049 million, to meet the annual debt service requirement for the 
General Obligation Refunding Bonds, Series 2013, originally issued for library capital projects.
52 of 763

--- page 53 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This GO Bond Tax Rate Levy is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is a government fiscal activity which does not 
involve any commitment to any specific project which may result in a potentially significant physical impact on the 
environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.
ATTACHMENTS
Att 1 - Proposed Resolution
Att 2 - GO Bonds Debt Service Tax Rate Calculation
Att 3 - Historic GO Bonds Tax Rates
Att 4 - GO Bonds Debt Service Schedule

Rich Lee, Director, Finance
rlee@cityofsanmateo.org

CITY OF SAN MATEO
RESOLUTION NO. ____ (2023)
FIXING THE 2023-2024 TAX RATE ON VOTER APPROVED GENERAL OBLIGATION BONDS
WHEREAS, adoption of California Constitution Article XIII A (Proposition 13) by the voters of the state
on June 6, 1978 automatically established the property tax rate to be fixed without any action of this Council, as
previously required by Charter Section 5.11 and California Government Code Section 51511, except voter 
approved bonds; and
WHEREAS, the City of San Mateo has voter-approved general obligation bonds outstanding, requiring
revenues in the sum of $2,049,050 to pay interest and principal for fiscal year 2023-24, which will require a total
tax rate of $0.0057 per $100 of assessed valuation for the Library Project on the estimated $36.2 billion of
combined assessed value roll of secured property and state utility property; and
WHEREAS, pursuant to California Constitution Article XIII A, all other revenue from property taxes
required to balance the budget for the City of San Mateo are to be collected by the County of San Mateo at a tax
rate of 1% of the full face value of real property located in the City of San Mateo ($1.00 per every $100 of
assessed value) and apportioned to the City according to law, and no other tax rate is to be established by City.
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, HEREBY RESOLVES 
AND ORDERS that: 
1.
In accordance with California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) Guidelines section 15378(b)(4), 
levying of this tax rate is not a project subject to CEQA because it is a funding mechanism which does not involve 
any commitment to any specific project that may result in a potentially significant physical impact on the 
environment.
2.
The rate of property tax to be levied by this Council for the fiscal year July 1, 2023 through June 30,
2024, upon each $100 assessed value of all taxable property within the City of San Mateo for the purpose of
redemption of interest and principal on voter-approved general obligation bonds of the City, for the fund, is
hereby fixed at:
General Obligation Bonds Fund – $0.0057 for the Library Project
3.
The City Clerk is hereby authorized and directed to forward a certified copy of this resolution to the
Controller of the County of San Mateo immediately, as the statement of tax rate fixed as required by law.

Drew Corbett, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Public Works Department
MEETING DATE:
June 05, 2023 
SUBJECT:
Wastewater Treatment Plant Supply of Sodium Bisulfite – Change Order
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve Contract Change Order No. 1 with Univar Solutions USA, Inc., to increase the supply of sodium bisulfite by 20,000 
gallons, for an amount not to exceed $32,340, for a total agreement amount of $355,734; and authorize the Public Works 
Director to execute the change order in substantially the form presented. 
BACKGROUND:
The Wastewater Treatment Plant, officially the Water Quality Control Plant, uses sodium bisulfite as a de-chlorinating 
reagent in the final wastewater treatment process before discharging to San Francisco Bay. The de-chlorination is a 
requirement of the City’s National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System permit. The winter of 2023 brought significant 
rains and abnormally high influent flows. These higher flows required more sodium bisulfite for de-chlorination. The fiscal 
year 2022-23 usage of sodium bisulfite is nearing its contract value. This contract change order will ensure adequate 
supply of sodium bisulfite and funds to provide appropriate de-chlorination for the remainder of the fiscal year.
BUDGET IMPACT:
There are sufficient funds in the Wastewater Treatment Plant operating budget for this change order. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This Contract Change Order No. 1 is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is a government fiscal activity which does not 
involve any commitment to any specific project which may result in a potentially significant physical impact on the 
environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.
ATTACHMENTS
Att 1 - Contract Change Order No. 1

DESCRIPTION OF WORK
Following is a description of work to be done, estimate of quantities, and prices to be paid.
The winter of 2023 brought significant rains and abnormally high influent flows. These higher flows required more sodium bisulfite for 
dechlorination. The FY2022-23 chemical budget for sodium bisulfite is nearing its contract value. This CCO will increase the total contract 
gallons from 200,000 gallons to 220,000 gallons. This additional 20,000 gallons will increase the total contract amount $32,340.00 
including tax, to ensure adequate funds to complete FY2022-23.  
Total cost of change not to exceed: $32,340.00

proposed and hereby agree, if this proposal is approved, that we will provide all 
equipment, furnish all materials, except as may otherwise be noted above, and 
perform all services necessary for the work above specified, and will accept as 
full payment therefore the prices shown above.

reagent in the treatment process. The chlorination is a requirement of the City’s National Pollutant Discharge Elimination 
System permit. The winter of 2023 brought significant rains and abnormally high influent flows. These higher flows 
required more sodium hypochlorite for disinfection. The fiscal year 2022-23 usage of sodium hypochlorite is nearing its 
contract value. This contract change order will ensure adequate supply of sodium hypochlorite and funding to provide

CITY OF SAN MATEO

TO:
City Council
FROM:
Drew Corbett, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Public Works Department
MEETING DATE:
June 05, 2023 
SUBJECT:
Wastewater Treatment Plant Supply of Chemicals – Contracts
RECOMMENDATION:
Award chemical supply contracts to: 1) Hasa, Inc. to supply sodium hypochlorite in the amount of $957,027; 2) Univar 
Solutions USA, Inc. to supply sodium bisulfite in the amount of $404,517; and 3) Kemira Water Solutions, Inc. for the 
supply of ferric chloride in the amount of $312,212; establish 10% contingency reserves for each contract; and authorize 
the Public Works Director to execute the contracts in substantially the form presented and issue change orders within the 
contingency amounts.
BACKGROUND:
The wastewater treatment plant (WWTP, officially the Water Quality Control Plant) uses chemicals to treat wastewater in 
accordance with applicable regulatory requirements. 
Sodium Hypochlorite 
The WWTP uses sodium hypochlorite as a disinfecting reagent in the chlorination treatment process. The chlorination 
process is a requirement of the City’s National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System (NPDES) permit that regulates 
discharge of treated wastewater to San Francisco Bay. This contract provides for supply and delivery of 300,000 gallons of 
this chemical product. The City participates with other municipalities in the Bay Area Chemical Consortium (BACC) to 
competitively bid the cost of chemicals needed for wastewater treatment. BACC bid results are summarized below: 
Contractor
Bid Amount
Contract Amount*
Hasa, Inc.
$2.91 
$873,000
Olin Corporation
$3.05
$915,000
    *Amount does not include 9.625% sales tax, see recommendation for amount including sales tax.
Based on the low bid of $2.91 per gallon, staff recommends awarding the contract to Hasa, Inc. for the supply of sodium 
hypochlorite. The contract term is 12 months, and the price is to remain firm for the entire period. 
Sodium Bisulfite 
The WWTP uses sodium bisulfite as a dechlorination reagent in the final wastewater treatment process prior to 
discharging to San Francisco Bay. The dechlorination process is a requirement of the City’s NPDES permit. This contract 
provides for supply and delivery of 200,000 gallons of this chemical product. The BACC bid result for sodium hypochlorite is 
summarized as follows: 
64 of 763

--- page 65 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Contractor
Bid Amount
Contract Amount*
 Univar Solutions USA, Inc.
$1.845
$369,000
 JCI Jones Chemicals Inc.
No Bid
No Bid
     * Amount does not include 9.625% sales tax, see recommendation for amount including sales tax.
Based on the sole bid of $1.845 per gallon, staff recommends awarding the contract to Univar Solutions USA, Inc. for the 
supply of sodium bisulfite. The contract term is 12 months, and the price is to remain firm for the entire period. 
Ferric Chloride 
The WWTP uses ferric chloride to control hydrogen sulfide formation, an odorous compound produced during the solids 
digestion process. Controlling hydrogen sulfide levels is a requirement of the Bay Area Air Quality Management District. 
This contract provides for supply and delivery of 200 dry tons of chemical product. The BACC bid result for ferric chloride is 
summarized below: 
Contractor
Bid Amount
Contract Amount*
Kemira Water Solutions, Inc.
$1,424
$284,800
Pencco, Inc.
$1,700
$340,000
Univar Solutions USA Inc.
No Bid
No Bid
    * Amount does not include 9.625% sales tax, see recommendation for amount including sales tax.
Based on the low bid of $1,424 per dry ton, staff recommends award of the contract to Kemira Water Solutions, Inc. for 
the supply of ferric chloride. The term of the contract is 12 months, and the price is to remain firm for the entire period.
BUDGET IMPACT:
There are sufficient funds for this agreement in the Fund 72 Sewer Enterprise operating budget for fiscal year 2023-24. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This contract is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is a government fiscal activity which does not involve any 
commitment to any specific project which may result in a potentially significant physical impact on the environment. 
(CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.
