SUBJECT:
100 N. Humboldt St. Property Owner Appeal – Statement of Decision 
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Statement of Decision granting the property owner’s appeal related to permanent relocation benefits to a tenant 
household who resided at 100 N. Humboldt Street. 
BACKGROUND:
On May 15, 2023, the City Council conducted a hearing on the property owner’s appeal from a notice compelling the 
property owner of 100 N. Humboldt Street to reimburse the City for permanent relocation benefits distributed to a tenant 
household due to unlawful substandard living conditions. The notice was issued pursuant to San Mateo Municipal Code 
Chapter 7.50 (Property Owner Obligations with Respect to Tenants Displaced from Unsafe or Substandard Units). The City 
Council granted the appeal and directed staff to prepare a written Statement of Decision for its adoption on the consent 
calendar at the June 5, 2023 City Council regular meeting. The written statement explaining the legal and factual basis for 
the Council's decision is included with this report as Attachment 1. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
The money used to provide relocation benefits by the City was from the Emergency Relocation Assistance Fund that was 
established to advance tenant assistance in urgent situations.  Because the appeal was granted, the funds provided by the 
City, approximately $13,000, will not be recouped. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This item is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity that will not result in 
direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.
ATTACHMENTS

1
Statement of Decision by the San Mateo City Council in the matter of the Property Owner Appeal of the 
Notice to Pay Relocation Benefits
(100 North Humboldt Street)
Section 1. Introduction
San Mateo Municipal Code (SMMC) Section 7.50.030(a) requires property owners to pay for the 
relocation of their tenants when the tenants are displaced by City code enforcement activities in response to 
substandard housing conditions.  SMMC Section 7.50.030(b) specifies instances where tenants are not 
considered displaced due to code enforcement activities, such as when the noncomplying condition(s) was 
created by the tenant household or the tenant’s guests and not created by the property owner, the tenant 
household unreasonably prevented the property owner from undertaking maintenance or repairs that would 
have prevented or rectified the noncomplying condition, or the conditions are solely from damage from an 
earthquake, fire, flood, natural disaster, civil disturbance or accident outside the control of the property owner 
and was not caused by acts or negligence of the property owner or due to pre-existing conditions or violations. 
SMMC Section 7.50.100 provides that property owners may contest a decision regarding relocation payments by 
appealing to the City Council.
In this case, on January 10, 2023, City Code Enforcement staff received a complaint of non-functional 
heaters, roof leaks, collapsed portions of ceiling in the kitchen and hallway, rats entering the home through 
holes in the roof and ceiling, and a lack of hot water reaching the kitchen sink and a bathroom shower at 100 
North Humboldt Street (the Property).  On January 13, 2023, Code Enforcement staff inspected the Property and 
observed numerous substandard conditions.  On January 26, 2023, staff subsequently issued a notice detailing 
eight (8) immediate life and safety conditions and 14 additional violations as a basis for requiring Trina Pierce 
(Property Owner) to correct the substandard conditions by February 27, 2023, requiring the Property to be 
vacated by January 30, 2023, and to pay the displaced tenants relocation benefits in the amount of $13,849 
(“Notice”).  The Property Owner and her attorney immediately contacted the City, at which point the City 
suspended the Notice pending further internal review and investigation.  
On January 30, 2023, City staff including Code Enforcement and Building staff, reinspected the Property.  
City Building staff determined the Property was uninhabitable and subsequently red-tagged the residence.  On 
January 31, 2023, the City received documentation from Property Owner’s attorney contesting the Notice and 
asserting the substandard conditions were generally created by the tenants, the Property Owner did not have 
notice of any of the substandard conditions, and the tenants’ complaints were motivated by a pending eviction.  
After a review of the most recent inspection, and documentation received from the Property Owner, the City 
amended its Notice detailing 10 immediate life safety conditions and 15 additional violations as a basis for 
requiring the Property Owner to correct the substandard conditions by February 27, 2023, requiring the 
Property to be immediately vacated, and to pay the tenants relocation benefits in the amount of $12,736.   Due 
to the red-tag and need to immediately vacate the residence, the City advanced relocation benefits to the 
tenant household as authorized by SMMC Section 7.50.070. The Property Owner appealed the Amended Notice, 
and after conducting a hearing on the matter, the City Council granted the appeal and directed staff to return to 
the Council with a Statement of Decision for formal adoption. 
SMMC Section 7.50.100(c)(1) requires, in relevant part, that “the City Council shall issue a written 
decision to uphold or cancel all or part of the decision, order, or determination regarding relocation benefits… 
and shall state the reasons for that decision,” and that “[t]he decision of the City Council shall include findings 
regarding the evidence in the record and submitted at the hearing, as well as the existence of any proper 
grounds for the order to pay relocation benefits or the notice of penalty or fine.” This statement of decision is 
393 of 763

--- page 394 ---
2
issued pursuant to these requirements.
Section 2. Applicable Law 
SMMC Section 7.50.030 provides, in relevant part, as follows:
a)
A tenant household shall be eligible for relocation payments from a property owner under this chapter if 
the tenant household is displaced from its dwelling or room due to the City's code enforcement 
activities. For purposes of this chapter, a tenant household shall be deemed to be displaced from its 
dwelling or room due to code enforcement activities in either of the following circumstances: 
1. 
The tenant household vacates its dwelling or room (whether or not the property owner requires 
vacation) after (a) the City or a court has issued a notice to vacate, notice to abate life-
threatening condition, or declaration of substandard condition covering that dwelling or room, 
and (b) the abatement period has expired without correction of the noncomplying condition (if a 
time period to abate the noncomplying condition is specified in such notice or declaration and 
the City or court does not order earlier vacation).
…
b)
Notwithstanding subsection (a) above, pursuant to subsection (b), a tenant household shall not be 
deemed displaced due to code enforcement activities in any of the following cases:
…
2.
The property owner demonstrates to the satisfaction of the Director that the noncomplying 
condition was created by the tenant household or the tenant household's guests or invitees, and 
was not created by the property owner or the property owner's agent, or that the condition 
giving rise to the code enforcement activities existed at the time that the tenant household 
occupied the dwelling or room and that the tenant household occupied the dwelling or room for 
the purpose of receiving relocation benefits;
3. 
The property owner demonstrates to the satisfaction of the Director that the tenant household 
unreasonably prevented the property owner or the property owner's agent from undertaking 
maintenance or repairs that would have prevented or rectified the noncomplying condition; or
…
7.
The tenant household is required to vacate the dwelling or room due solely to damage resulting 
from an earthquake, fire, flood, natural disaster, civil disturbance, or accident outside the 
control of the property owner, if (a) the vacation is required within six months of such event, 
and (b) the property owner demonstrates to the satisfaction of the Director that such damage 
was not caused by the acts or the negligence of the property owner or by a preexisting condition 
in the building in violation of applicable building, housing, fire, or other health and safety codes.
SMMC Section 7.50.070 provides, in relevant part, as follows:
(a) The City, in its sole discretion and subject to funding availability, may make any of the payments 
required of a property owner under this chapter, including advancing eligible tenant household(s) 
reimbursable "moving expenses" and "temporary housing accommodations costs" as each of these 
terms are defined below in section 7.50.090(b). Such payments shall continue to be an obligation of the 
property owner and shall be reimbursed by the property owner to the City. The City may consider 
making such payments in its own discretion or if a tenant household makes a written request to the 
Department following a property owner's failure to pay the required payments within the period 
mandated under section 7.50.060, but in no event later than sixty (60) days following the tenant 
394 of 763

--- page 395 ---
3
household's vacation of the dwelling or room. Prior to any City payment to a tenant household, the 
Director shall make a determination regarding the eligibility of the tenant household for relocation 
payments. The Department will make reasonable efforts to contact a representative of the property 
owner prior to making the determination or authorizing the City's payment. However, failure to give 
prior notice to the property owner shall not relieve the property owner of any obligations under this 
chapter.
…
SMMC Section 7.50.020 provides as follows:
“Permanent displacement” means the vacating of a dwelling or room by a tenant household due to code 
enforcement activities when that dwelling or room (or an equivalent dwelling or room in the building or 
complex), in the judgment of the Enforcement Officer, cannot foreseeably be brought into code compliance or 
will not otherwise be available for re-occupancy by the tenant household within ninety (90) days from the date 
of vacating; or when the tenant household and the property owner have agreed that the displacement shall be 
permanent.
"Temporary displacement" means the vacating of a dwelling or room by a tenant household due to code 
enforcement activities when that dwelling or room (or an equivalent dwelling or room in the same building or 
complex) will foreseeably be brought into code compliance and be available for re-occupancy by the tenant 
household within ninety (90) days from the date of vacating; or when the tenant household and property owner 
have otherwise agreed that the displacement shall be considered temporary.
"Tenant household" means one or more individuals who rent or lease a dwelling or room as their 
primary residence and who share living expenses.
Section 3. Evidence Presented
At the hearing on this matter, City staff presented documentary evidence and testimony to establish the 
following:
a)
The Property is a three-bedroom single family home with 10 occupants listed on the lease 
agreement, not including an unknown number of children (collectively “Tenants” or “Tenant 
Household”).  
b)
On January 10, 2023, Code Enforcement staff received a complaint of substandard conditions 
existing at the Property consisting of non-functional heaters, roof leaks for over one year and tarp 
being used instead of repairs, collapsed portions of ceiling in the kitchen and hallway, rats entering 
the home through holes in the roof and ceiling, and a lack of hot water reaching the kitchen sink and 
a bathroom shower despite the Property Owner replacing the water heater.
c)
On January 13, 2023, Code Enforcement staff inspected the Property and observed the following 
immediate life safety conditions:
1.
Visible mold in both the walls and ceilings located in the bathroom and in two bedrooms.
2.
Lack of hot water reaching the minimum temperature of 110 degrees.
3.
Lack of required heat in the dwelling.
4.
Multiple electrical hazards throughout the house.

4
5.
Multiple ceiling and roof holes causing water leaks and damage throughout the dwelling.
6.
Lack of required smoke detectors in the entire dwelling.
7.
Rodents/pests in the dwelling.
8.
Termite damage.
On January 26, 2023, Code Enforcement subsequently issued a Warning Letter to Abate a Public 
Nuisance and Declaration of Substandard Dwelling Condition and Notice to Vacate (“Notice”) to the 
Property Owner detailing the observed substandard conditions requiring compliance by February 
27, 2023, ordering vacation of the residence by January 30, 2023, and ordering the Property Owner 
to pay the tenants permanent relocation benefits in the amount of $13,849 because the Property 
could not foreseeably be brought into code compliance within 90 days from the date of the Notice 
due to the extensive violations.  
d)
After discussions with the Property Owner and her attorney, Code Enforcement staff suspended the 
Notice pending further review and investigation.
e)
On January 30, 2023, Code Enforcement and Building staff re-inspected the Property and observed 
the following immediate life safety conditions: 
1.
Visible mold in both the walls and ceilings located in the bathroom and in two bedrooms.
2.
Lack of hot water reaching the minimum temperature of 110 degrees.
3.
Lack of required heat in the dwelling.
4.
Multiple electrical hazards throughout the house.
5.
Multiple ceiling and roof holes causing water leaks and damage throughout the dwelling.
6.
Lack of required smoke detectors in the entire dwelling.

issued a red-tag prohibiting any entry and occupation of the Property.

from the Property Owner’s attorney alleging the substandard conditions were generally created by 
the tenants, the Property Owner did not have notice of any of the substandard conditions, and the 
tenants’ complaints were motivated by a pending eviction.
g)
On February 1, 2023, the City issued an Amended Notice detailing the observed immediate life 
safety conditions observed during the reinspection on January 31, 2023, and requiring the Property 
Owner to correct all substandard conditions by February 27, 2023, requiring the Property to be 
immediately vacated, and to pay the tenants permanent relocation benefits in the amount of

2.
An immediate vacation allowance of $1,000.
3.
The total sum of the permanent relocation costs in the amount of $12,736.

construction (including demolition), and to schedule an inspection with Code Enforcement.
n)
On April 21, 2023, the City received a mold report, dated March 8, 2023, from the Property Owner 
confirming the presence of mold and mold abatement recommendations.

On May 5, 2023, the City reinspected the Property and found ongoing unpermitted work occurring.

On May 9, 2023, the City issued a Stop Work Notice due to the unpermitted work.

For purposes of the Appeal Hearing, the City pursued reimbursement for permanent relocation

1.
Visible mold on walls and ceilings of the bathroom and two bedrooms.
2.
Lack of hot water reaching 110 degrees.
3.
Lack of required hearing in the dwelling.
4.
Multiple ceiling and roof holes causing water leaks and damage throughout the dwelling.
5.
Rodents/pests in dwelling.
Section 4. Defenses Asserted
At the hearing on this matter, the Property Owner argued that she was not required to pay the Tenants 
any permanent relocation costs on the following grounds:
a)
The Property was in good condition when the Tenants moved in and the Property Owner responded 
appropriately to needed repairs.
b)
The Tenants were responsible for damage to the Property.
397 of 763

--- page 398 ---
6
c)
The Property Owner was not made aware of the uninhabitable conditions described in the Notice.
d)
The Tenants actively concealed the uninhabitable conditions described in the Notice from the 
Property Owner. 
e)
The Property Owner appropriately addressed the roof leaks and was in the process of having the 
roof repaired. 
f)
The Tenants were motivated by a pending eviction, and their complaints and submitted evidence 
was not credible due to this fact.
g)
The City should have considered the pending eviction when reviewing the weight of the evidence.
h)
The City should have been more communicative with the Property Owner when it issued the Notice 
and Amended Notice.
Section 5. Findings of Fact
Based upon the evidence presented at the hearing on this matter, the City Council makes the following 
findings of fact:
a)
The Property is a three-bedroom single family home with ten adult occupants, and an unknown 
number of children.
b)
On October 26, 2022, the Property Owner received a quote for a new roof and was in the planning 
stages of repairing the roof.  The Property Owner’s temporary patches to the dwelling and use of a 
tarp to cover the roof were adequate mitigation measures to address a leaking roof and collapsed 
portions of ceiling pending scheduling of a permanent repair.
c)
On November 17, 2022, the Property Owner served a 60-day notice to vacate to the Tenants.
d)
On January 10, 2023, Code Enforcement staff received a complaint of substandard conditions 
existing at the Property consisting of non-functional heaters, roof leaks for over one year and tarp 
being used instead of repairs, collapsed portions of ceiling in the kitchen and hallway, rats entering 
the home through holes in the roof and ceiling, and a lack of hot water reaching the kitchen sink and 
a bathroom shower despite the Property Owner replacing the water heater.
e)
On January 13, 2023, Code Enforcement staff inspected the Property and observed the following 
immediate life safety conditions:
1.
Visible mold in both the walls and ceilings located in the bathroom and in two bedrooms.
2.
Lack of hot water reaching the minimum temperature of 110 degrees.
3.
Lack of required heat in the dwelling.
4.
Multiple electrical hazards throughout the house.
5.
Multiple ceiling and roof holes causing water leaks and damage throughout the dwelling.
6.
Lack of required smoke detectors in the entire dwelling.
7.
Rodents/pests in the dwelling.
8.
Termite damage.

Based upon the findings above, the City Council, by a 3-1 vote, grants the appeal of the Property Owner 
and finds the Property Owner is not required to reimburse the City for permanent relocation benefits advanced 
to the Tenants in the amount of $12,736.

--- page 404 ---
From:
Peninsula People Power
To:
Clerk
Cc:
City Council (San Mateo)
Subject:
100 N Humboldt St. Statement of Appeal
Date:
Monday, June 5, 2023 3:59:35 PM
Dear City Council,
We recently learned about a troubling situation in San Mateo where a Latino family of 10 (not
including the children) was living in unsafe conditions. The family contacted the City after a
portion of the ceiling collapsed. City officials inspected the property and discovered 22 code
violations – 10 of which were life-safety violations – including no heat, no hot water, a
compromised roof and significant mold.
The city quickly acted in accordance with their red-tag ordinance passed in 2019, issuing a
notice to vacate and providing the tenants with relocation assistance. The city then sent the
property owner a bill for reimbursement of the displacement benefits provided to the tenants.
The property owner appealed the bill, alleging that the code violations were caused by the
tenants. The property owner provided no evidence that the tenants caused the lack of heat and
hot water, roof damage or environmental mold. On the other hand, the tenants asserted that the
property was rented without heat and provided communications demonstrating they had raised
concerns about the 37 year old roof as early as 2020. Google Street View images from 2018
clearly show damage to the exterior of the roof. The property owner obtained an estimate to
replace the roof in October 2022 but never proceeded with the work. The owner replaced the
water heater in May 2022, but the work was completed without a permit and the tenants said it
did not work reliably. City inspectors found the air and water temperature were both about
60℉.
As you know, Council voted to grant the property owner’s appeal by a vote of 3-1. Deputy
Mayor Nash was not convinced the life-safety violations were caused by the owner.
Councilmember Hedges said that the owner placing tarps on the roof was sufficient mitigation,
and that it was probably hard to find a roofer to complete the repairs. Councilmember
Newsom said “without a doubt" there was negligence with regards to the roof that warranted
red-tagging the property and relocating the tenants, but voted to grant the appeal anyway.
We strongly disagree with the City Council’s decision and believe it sets a dangerous
precedent. We worry that the most vulnerable residents living in uninhabitable conditions will
hesitate to raise concerns to their landlords or the city, for fear of displacement without
assistance.
We urge the Council to reconsider their decision, and act to protect the 46% of San Mateo
residents that are renters from irresponsible and negligent landlords. The red-tag ordinance is a
critical safety net for renters trapped in unsafe conditions and it must not be weakened.
Respectfully,
Peninsula People Power
REACH Coalition of San Mateo County

FROM:
Drew Corbett, City Manager

Public Works Department
MEETING DATE:
June 05, 2023 
SUBJECT:
Sewer Rate Increase for Fiscal Years 2023-24 to 2027-28 – Ordinance Introduction
RECOMMENDATION:
Conduct a public hearing, and if there is a determination that a majority protest does not exist, introduce an ordinance to 
establish maximum sewer rates effective August 1, 2023 through June 30, 2028 pursuant to San Mateo Municipal Code 
Section 3.54.030 “Sewer Service Charges.” 
BACKGROUND:
Council establishes rates sufficient to pay for sewer collection and treatment operating expenses, debt service, and capital 
improvement needs. On March 5, 2018, Council adopted a multi-year sewer rate increase and rate restructure for fiscal 
years 2018-19 through 2022-23, which included moving from charging solely based on volumetric usage to a hybrid 
volumetric/fixed-fee structure to help stabilize revenue. The multi-year rate increase and fee restructuring allowed the 
City to finance the Clean Water Program (CWP), a $1B capital intensive 10-year effort that began in 2015 to comply with a 
Regional Water Quality Control Board Cease and Desist Order and National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System permit 
requirements. 
The CWP consists of a series of projects that upgrade and replace aging infrastructure, increase system capacity to prevent 
sanitary sewer overflows, and are designed to meet current and future regulatory requirements for wastewater 
treatment. The sewer rates have funded operating costs and the CWP through cash, bonds, and loans. Low-interest federal 
Water Infrastructure Finance and Innovation Act (WIFIA) and State Revolving Fund loans were obtained, saving rate payers 
millions of dollars and reducing the need for higher future rate increases. 
During an April 2022 study session, Council supported a multi-year sewer rate schedule consisting of inflationary-level 
increases to ensure sufficient ongoing revenue to fund operating and maintenance costs and capital improvement 
projects. Following the study session, staff worked with the City’s sewer rate consultant, Bartle Wells, to determine 
recommended sewer rates for meeting the City’s ongoing needs. At an April 3, 2023 study session, Council provided 
feedback on a proposed five-year inflationary sewer rate structure, up to a maximum 3% increase each year for fiscal years 
2023-24 through 2027-28. Council also provided feedback on a draft Proposition 218 Notice of proposed sewer rate 
increases.
Proposition 218 Compliance
Increases to the City’s sewer service charges must comply with Article XIIID of the California Constitution (Proposition 218). 
Proposition 218 imposes various requirements regarding the procedures the City must follow to increase its sewer service 
fees as well as substantive limitations on the sewer service charges. Proposition 218 requires the City to mail a written 
notice of any proposed sewer rate increases to affected property owners at least 45 days prior to a scheduled public 
hearing, during which time property owners may submit written protests opposing proposed rate increases. 
406 of 763

--- page 407 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
The Proposition 218 Notice (Attachment 2) was mailed to 27,103 property owners on April 13, 2023, allowing for a 53-day 
protest period. The notification informed property owners of the hearing date on which the proposed maximum rate 
increases would be considered by the Council. The notice also instructed property owners on how, when, and where to 
submit protests. 
As described in the notice, a valid protest must be presented in writing and received prior to the end of the public hearing 
and include the required information.  At the end of the public hearing, the City Clerk will tally and report the qualifying 
written protests. The Council will then determine whether the written protests opposing the proposed rate increases 
exceeds the fifty (50) percent threshold required to constitute a majority protest. 
If there is not a majority protest on the proposed rate increases, Council may proceed to introduce the proposed 
ordinance (Attachment 1) to amend sewer rates. A second reading and adoption of the ordinance would then occur on 
June 20, 2023 to establish the new sewer service rates for fiscal years 2023-24 to 2027-28. The rates adopted by Council 
may not exceed the maximum rates shown in Attachment 1. The proposed rate adjustment for fiscal years 2023-24 will 
become effective August 1, 2023.  Rate adjustments for fiscal years 2024-25 to 2027-28 would be effective July 1st of each 
fiscal year.
BUDGET IMPACT:
The proposed rate increases for the fiscal years 2023-24 to 2027-28 are calculated based on the projected revenue 
requirements to provide sewer service and cover the City’s costs, including debt service, required reserves, and future 
capital expenditures. The actual revenue to be generated will not be known until the rate is set and complete water data is 
available and analyzed. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
In accordance with CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4), the adoption of this ordinance is not a project subject to CEQA, 
because the sewer service charge is a funding mechanism for the City Sewer operations and capital projects.
NOTICE PROVIDED
Notice of the proposed maximum increase in the sewer rates and the June 5, 2023 public hearing was mailed to all 
property owners on April 13, 2023.  This meets the 45-day notification requirement. Pursuant to Government Code Section 
6062a, notice of this public hearing was published twice in a newspaper, on May 24, 2023, and May 31, 2023, respectively.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
DRAFT ORDINANCE
ESTABLISHING SEWER SERVICE CHARGES FOR FISCAL YEARS 2023-2024 THROUGH 2027-2028
WHEREAS, the procedures set forth in California Constitution Article XIII D, Section 6 for property-
related fees, enacted by Proposition 218, apply to sewer service charge increases; and
WHEREAS, a sewer service charge is a property-related fee for each parcel’s attributable use of the 
sanitary sewer collection and treatment systems in the City (“Sewer Service Charge”); and
WHEREAS, the City is following the procedures set forth in Article XIII D, Section 6 for the proposed 
Sewer Service Charge increase; and
WHEREAS, notice of the proposed Sewer Service Charge increase was mailed to ratepayers and property 
owners on April 13, 2023, 53 days prior to the scheduled public hearing on the proposed increase; and
WHEREAS, on June 5, 2023, a public hearing was held at 7:00 p.m. in the San Mateo City Hall Council 
Chambers, located at 330 West 20th Avenue, San Mateo regarding the proposed Sewer Service Charge increase, 
at which all protests against the rate increase and other comments were considered; and
WHEREAS, San Mateo Municipal Code section 3.54.030 provides that the City Council shall establish the 
Sewer Service Charge by ordinance.
NOW, THEREFORE, THE COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO ORDAINS AS FOLLOWS:
Section 1. In accordance with California Constitution Article XIII D, Section 6, the City Council finds that:
(a) The revenues derived from the Sewer Service Charge do not exceed the funds required to provide 
sewer service because the rate increase is needed to fund ongoing sewer system operation and maintenance 
and meet capital expenditures.
(b) The revenues derived from the Sewer Service Charge will not be used for any purpose other than the 
provision of sewer service.
(c) The Sewer Service Charge will not exceed the cost of sewer service attributable to each parcel.
(d) The Sewer Service Charge is only charged for sewer service that is actually used by or currently 
available to the parcel in question.
(e) The Sewer Service Charge pays for the provision of sewer service and not for general governmental 
services.
Section 2. Pursuant to San Mateo Municipal Code section 3.54.030, the maximum sewer service charge for Fiscal 
Years 2023-2024 through 2027-2028 is established as follows:

Section 3. Pursuant to San Mateo County requirements, the City Council authorizes collection of the Sewer 
Service Charge, which shall be billed on the real property tax rolls for Fiscal Year 2023-2024 through 2027-2028, 
with the actual total to be updated for the County rolls when the calculation process is completed each year.
Section 4. Environmental Determination. In accordance with California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) 
Guidelines, section 15378(b)(4), introduction and adoption of this Ordinance is not a project subject to CEQA 
because the Sewer Service Charge is a funding mechanism for the City sewer operations and capital projects 
which does not involve any commitment to any specific project that may result in a potentially significant 
physical impact on the environment.
Section 5. Severability. In the event any section, clause or provision of this ordinance shall be determined 
invalid or unconstitutional, such section, clause or provision shall be deemed severable and all other sections or 
portions hereof shall remain in full force and effect.
Section 6. Publication. This Ordinance shall be published in summary in a newspaper of general circulation, 
posted in the City Clerk's Office, and posted on the City's website, all in accord with Section 2.15 of the City 
Charter.
Section 7. Legislative History and Effective Date. This ordinance was introduced on Clerk to complete., and 
adopted on Clerk to complete., and shall be effective 30 days after its adoption.

Comments and Written Protests
Any affected property owner may submit a
written protest to the proposed increase to
the sewer rates; provided, however, only
one protest will be counted per parcel. Each
protest must (1) be in writing; (2) state that
the identified property owner opposes the
proposed increases to the sewer rates; (3)
identify the parcel for which the protest is
made (by assessor’s parcel number or street
address); and (4) include the signature of the
property owner. Protests submitted by e-mail,
facsimile, or other electronic means will not be
counted. Written protests may be submitted by
mail to the City Clerk at 330 W. 20th Avenue,
San Mateo, CA 94403 or at the public hearing,
so long as the protests are received before
the public hearing ends. Please identify on
the envelope for any written protest, whether
mailed or submitted in person to the City Clerk,
that the enclosed written protest is for the Public
Hearing on the Proposed Sewer Rate Increase.
Notice of Public Hearing
on Proposed Sewer Rate
Increases
This notice is being sent to inform you that the
City of San Mateo is proposing to increase
sewer rates over the next five years in order
to a) continue constructing a new wastewater
treatment plant to replace the City’s aging
treatment facility, b) fund legally-required
improvements to the City’s sewer collection
system to address regulatory requirements and
minimize potential for sanitary sewer overflows,
c) fund ongoing rehabilitation of aging sewer
pipelines, and d) keep rates aligned with the
cost of providing sewer service for fiscal year
(FY) 2023-24 to FY 2027-28. The rates reflect
maximum annual rates over the five-year
period.
During the public hearing, the City Council
will hear all public testimony and consider
all written protests. At the conclusion of the
public hearing, the City Council will decide
whether to adopt the proposed sewer rates.
Oral comments at the public hearing will not
qualify as formal protests unless accompanied
by a written protest, but the City welcomes
your input. If, at the end of the public hearing,
written protests against the proposed increases
to the sewer rates are not presented by a
majority of affected property owners of the
identified parcels, the City Council will be
authorized to adopt the rates.
In compliance with Article XIIID of the California
State Constitution and the Proposition 218
Omnibus Implementation Act, the City is
notifying all affected property owners of a
Public Hearing to accept public comment
& testimony on the proposed sewer rate
increases:
Date: June 5, 2023 at 7:00 p.m.
San Mateo City Hall,
Council Chambers
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo, CA 94403
Need More Information? Please visit our
website, CleanWaterProgramSanMateo.org.
For questions, call (650) 727-6870 or email info@
CleanWaterProgramSanMateo.org.

--- page 411 ---
The City’s current sewer rates for a typical residential
customer are in the middle range compared to other
regional agencies in San Mateo County. The rates for fiscal
years 2023-24 through 2027-28 reflect the maximum annual
rate increase proposed under this Proposition 218 notice.
The City will review the rates each year and determine the
actual annual increase up to these maximums.
Why the Rate Increases Are Needed
Methodology Used to Calculate the Proposed Rates
Specifically, the proposed rate increases will enable the City
to generate the funding needed to:
• Continue construction and long-term financing of the City’s
aging wastewater treatment plant
The proposed sewer rates are based on a cost-of-service
analysis that includes a) financial projections to identify future
wastewater system funding needs, and b) apportionment
of costs and development of rates for each customer class
based on factors including the volume and strength of
wastewaterdischarge.
The City’s existing wastewater treatment plant includes
several facilities which have reached the end of useful life.
The treatment plant is being rebuilt to ensure the City can
continue to provide high-quality service and meet new
regulatoryrequirements to protect San Francisco Bay.
Approved sewer rates for 2023-24 would be effective August
1 and effective July 1 for each subsequent fiscal year under
the proposed rate structure. Please note sewer charges for
most customers are billed annually on the property tax bill.
The proposed rates are designed to recover the costs of
providing sewer service to each parcel.
Proposed Sewer Rates
• Construct critical improvements to the City’s sewer
collection system to meet regulatory requirements, prevent
sanitary sewer overflows, and replace aging pipelines
The City needs to address increased regulatory
Sample Bill Calculations
The following table shows current and proposed sewer
rates. Residential rates include two components:
Sample Residential Monthly Sewer Charge
Effective August 1, 2023
1) A Fixed Monthly Charge per residential dwelling unit that is
billed independent of usage, and 2) Sewer Usage Charges
billed based on metered water usage. Commercial and non-
residential customers will continue to be billed Sewer Usage
Charges based on customer class and metered usage,
subject to a minimum charge. Residential and commercial
Sewer Usage Charges are based on metered water usage
from the preceding November through March time period
(when there is minimal outdoor irrigation) and levied per
hundred cubic feet (CCF). One CCF equals approximately
748 gallons.
requirements and issues associated with sanitary sewer
overflow prevention, including construction of relief sewer
lines and facilities necessary to prevent overflows during
heavy rains, as well as increased cleaning, inspection, and
rehabilitation of the existing collection system.

underhanded way to convince us that this was a good project to approve!!!)

the $1,827 that we pay now. 
I am struggling to get by due to the nonstop obsession of the City and CA pushing more 
development and creating a hostile business environment altogether, something it is 
exasperating to fight. We need common sense approaches in our public sector that the 
private sector has to live within. The current administration policies and anti business 
environment in CA has cost us dearly with increases in gas, food, garbage rates, water rates 
and PG&E (rate hikes seem to always be approved) and  all continuing to increase without 
thought to   those paying the bills/taxes.

City Council members do not want any input from the people who live under their tyranny.  
They are elected by the citizens of San Mateo and we have a right to be heard and freely 
comment on what the City Council proposes…as far as I understand, that is still a right our 
Constitution gives to “we the people.”   
Please vote No on the proposed sewer rate increase. 
Sincerely,

higher water bills, garbage rates, gas at the pump, PG&E rates and cost of food increases.  We

CITY OF SAN MATEO
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
Agenda Number:  30
Section Name: PUBLIC HEARING
Account Number: 10-2031
File ID: 23-7548
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Drew Corbett, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Finance Department
MEETING DATE:
June 05, 2023 
SUBJECT:
Proposed 2023-24 Budget and 2023-28 Capital Improvement Program – Review
RECOMMENDATION:
Review the proposed 2023-24 budget and the 2023-28 capital improvement program and conduct a public hearing prior to 
the scheduled adoption at the June 20, 2023 City Council meeting.
BACKGROUND:
Prior to Council’s adoption of the 2023-24 budget on June 20, 2023, it holds two public hearings to receive public comment 
on the proposed budget and capital improvement program (CIP). Council was presented with a preview of the General 
Fund proposed 2023-24 budget on April 17, 2023. Overall, the proposed Citywide budget for 2023-24 is $317.5 million. 
This includes an operating budget of $239.1 million and a capital budget of $78.4 million. A summary of budget highlights 
is included in the City Manager’s Letter of Budget Transmittal in the Proposed 2023-24 Budget Book, included as 
Attachment 1. This agenda report primarily focuses on the General Fund, as it accounts for major tax revenues and funds 
the City's core services. Additional narratives are provided for the Construction Services Fund, as it is a perennial major 
fund of the City’s operations, and the Sewer Fund, which is the City’s sole enterprise fund and the second largest fund of 
the City. Fund summaries for all other operating funds are provided in the Proposed 2023-24 Budget Book. Narratives will 
be provided to discuss service-level enhancements as reflected in the 2023-24 position budget, but the focus of fiscal year 
(FY) 2023-24 is on the capital improvement program (CIP), as FY 2023-24 is year two of the two-year business plan. As 
such, the Proposed 2023-24 Budget Book includes summaries for the 2023-28 CIP as well as charters for funded projects 
and priority unfunded projects. This agenda report provides a high-level summary. The Proposed 2023-24 Budget Book 
also includes operating budget schedules to provide greater detail.
GENERAL FUND 
The General Fund, which is the City's primary operating fund, projects total revenues of $159.1 million and total 
expenditures of $171.7 million and includes transfers out and all General Fund sub-funds – Measure S, Downtown 
Services, Recreation Services, and Golf. Budget assumptions for major General Fund tax revenues are discussed in greater 
detail below. 
GENERAL FUND REVENUES 
PROPERTY TAX 
The proposed 2022-23 budget anticipates $78.0 million in property tax revenues, which is 45% of General Fund total 
revenues, and as such, is the General Fund’s chief revenue source. The primary components of property tax revenues 
include secured current year ($49.2 million), Excess ERAF ($8.2 million), and property tax in lieu of VLF ($10.8 million). 
These primary property tax components grow annually based on the change in assessed value, which as of May 2023, is 
projected to be 4.65%, which includes the change in the California Consumer Price Index (CCPI) or 2%, whichever is less, 
pursuant to the State constitution. The remainder of the growth in assessed value is attributable to properties that have 
425 of 763

--- page 426 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
been sold and the applicable assessed value reset to market value, as well as new development that has been added to the 
City’s property tax roll. The proposed 2023-24 budget anticipates that property tax will be $2.2 million less than the 
adopted 2022-23 budget. The primary culprit in the year-over-year reduction is property tax in-lieu of vehicle license fees 
(VLF). 
As a part of the 2004 budget agreement, the State of California agreed to swap the loss of vehicle license fees (otherwise 
known as VLF) with property tax. These property taxes have two funding sources: 1) property taxes from non-basic aid 
school districts, and 2) ERAF that would otherwise go to non-basic aid school districts. Due to the preponderance of basic 
aid school districts in the county (17 of 23), there are not enough non-basic aid school districts to backfill the VLF owed to 
the cities and the County. As such, the County of San Mateo has experienced property tax in lieu of VLF shortfalls over the 
past couple of years, which is an issue that is unique to San Mateo County. The procedure to address the VLF shortfall has 
been for the County to file a claim with the State at the end of the fiscal year of the VLF shortfall, which is taken into 
consideration by the State for the subsequent year’s budget. The county-wide VLF shortfall from fiscal year (FY) 2021-22 of 
$33 million is not included in the State’s proposed FY 2023-24 budget, despite advocacy efforts from San Mateo County 
and the local cities. The City’s share of the FY 2022-23 county-wide shortfall is $1.9 million. In addition, the proposed 2023-
24 budget assumes a county-wide VLF shortfall of $78.5 million, of which the City’s share would be $4.6 million. Absent the 
2021-22 VLF shortfall and the projected 2023-24 VLF shortfall, property tax revenues would be $84.0 million.
SALES TAX 
The proposed 2023-24 budget assumes Local 1% sales tax revenues (Bradley Burns) of $23.9 million and local 1/4% sales 
tax revenues (Measure S) of $7.3 million, which reflects modest growth of the City’s sales tax base after recovering back to 
the pre-pandemic baseline within a two-year period in FY 2021-22. Despite the relatively rapid recovery, the ongoing shift 
of consumer habits of purchasing taxable goods online rather than physical stores is expected to continue to keep sales tax 
growth at a modest pace, as the Bradley Burns is allocated based on point of sale. 
By contrast, Measure S, which is a 1/4% transactions and use tax, otherwise known as a district tax, is allocated based on 
destination. While the Bradley Burns and district tax are based on different tax laws, the disparity between the amount of 
revenue generated between the two sales tax revenues relative to their rate illustrates the impact of the gradual changes 
in consumer behavior. If the Bradley Burns tax were modified to be in alignment with modern technology and current 
consumer habits by being destination based, Bradley Burns sales tax revenues would be closer to $28.7 million rather than 
$23.5 million, a $5.2 million difference. 
REAL PROPERTY TRANSFER TAX 
The federal government’s efforts to mitigate high inflation by increasing the federal funds rate increases the cost to 
borrow money. These actions at the federal level have a stifling effect on the real estate market, which in turn has caused 
the City’s real property transfer tax (RPTT) to precipitously drop. The adopted 2022-23 budget assumed RPTT revenues of 
$11.1 million based on an analysis of RPTT trends over the current and previous economic cycles. As a result of the 
escalation of the federal funds rate, RPTT revenue for 2022-23 are projected to end the year at $6.3 million. Over the next 
couple of years, the Federal Open Market Committee anticipates that the federal funds rate will begin to taper off. As 
such, the proposed 2023-24 budget and the General Fund Long-Term Financial Plan assume an average of $8.0 million 
throughout the 10-year period. The Letter of Transmittal in the Proposed 2023-24 Budget Book discusses the current trend 
in greater detail.
TRANSIENT OCCUPANCY TAX
Local tourism and transient occupancy taxes (TOT) revenues have recovered faster than expected, but TOT remains the 
only tax revenue that has not yet recovered back to pre-pandemic levels. The proposed 2023-24 budget assumes that TOT 
revenues will reach $6.0 million. San Mateo’s TOT base is largely from business travel, which is anticipated to recover back 
to the pre-pandemic baseline by FY 2025-26 as reflected in the General Fund Long-Term Financial Plan based on analysis 
from Tourism Economics and Visit California. 
426 of 763

--- page 427 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
GENERAL FUND EXPENDITURES 
The proposed 2023-24 budget projects $171.7 million in General Fund total expenditures, which primarily consists of $86.7 
million in employee compensation, which includes salaries, wages, healthcare, and pension costs. Employee compensation 
represents over half of total General Fund expenditures and reflects the City’s ongoing commitment to provide 
competitive employee compensation and the proportional demand on General Fund resources. FY 2023-24 is year two of 
the two-year business plan, so the focus of the budget is on the CIP. Modest service level enhancements added $1.4 
million in additional cost to the operating budget to address key operational areas, including planning, building, traffic, 
engineering, and wastewater, as detailed below:
Community Development Department
•
1.0 full-time equivalent (FTE) Principal Planner - $213,000; Allocated 25% to Advance Planning Fund and 75% 
Construction Services Fund
o
This position is needed to ensure the timely handling of the increased number of planning applications in 
accordance with State law, ensure the City achieves and remains in compliance with the high volume of 
new State land use laws, which expedite processing times and require policy and code changes, assist with 
and lead Housing Element and General Plan implementation tasks,  including the comprehensive Zoning 
Code update, and assist the Planning Manager with streamlining permit processing and supporting staff 
with handing current planning tasks.
•
1.0 FTE Deputy Building Official - $197,000; Allocated 10% to General Fund and 90% to Construction Services Fund
o
This position is needed to assist the Building Official with process improvements and help support staff on 
technical matters and complex customer service issues related to the approximately 4,000 permits 
processed each year, and which is increasing in volume and turnaround times commensurately with 
planning applications. The Building Division comprises approximately half of the Department’s staff and 
has not had a proportional amount of management support. Adding back this previously funded position 
into the organizational structure would provide the support needed to channel some of CDD’s resources 
toward staff mentorship and operational improvements to improve customer service and shorten permit 
processing and response times.
•
1.0 FTE Development Review Technician - $120,000; Allocated 100% to Construction Services Fund
o
This position is needed to field the high volume of building and planning inquiries we experience daily and 
process the intake of building and planning permits submitted, which continue to increase. Development 
Review Technicians are difficult to attract and retain, as compensation in nearby cities is competitive, 
while work volumes often appear to be lower. Further, firms that offer professional services often do not 
have staff available to fill this role, making it difficult to obtain help when there are unexpected vacancies 
in our DRT pool. Adding this position would provide for a more balanced workload when fully staffed, 
allowing us to focus on process improvements, and help to ensure customer levels can be maintained  
when we experience staffing shortages, which is not unusual for this position.
Public Works Department
•
1.0 FTE Traffic and Lighting Technician - $138,000; Allocated 100% to General Fund
o
The City has 90 traffic signals and 6,700 streetlights that are currently maintained by one Traffic and 
Lighting Technician and one Traffic Safety Worker. For traffic infrastructure of this size, the Federal 
Highway Administration (FHWA) recommends a staff of three Traffic and Lighting Technicians and two 
Traffic Safety Workers. While still short of the FHWA recommendations, an additional Traffic and Lighting 
Technician would help address the maintenance requirements to ensure the safe operation of the traffic 
infrastructure.  
•
1.0 FTE Associate Engineer - $168,000; Allocated 65% to General Fund and 35% to Construction Services Fund
o
The Traffic Engineering group within Public Works is responsible for improving the operation of the City’s 
traffic infrastructure through the optimization of traffic signal timing for congestion relief; addressing 
safety concerns through the development of speed studies and the implementation of traffic calming 
projects; review of collision data for the development of traffic safety improvements; development of 
capital improvement projects such as the 101/Peninsula Interchange, Speed Limit Zone Reductions, High 
Voltage, and 19th Ave. / Fashion Island Traffic Improvements; and review of traffic impact analyses for new 
427 of 763

--- page 428 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
development.  In addition, the group is responsible for leading studies, such as the Local Roadway Safety 
Plan, Traffic Signal Master Plan, and the Circulation Element of the General Plan. The group consists of one 
Senior Engineer, two Associate Engineers, an Engineering Technician and is resource limited, unable to 
address resident requests and the implementation of critical safety projects in a timely and efficient 
manner. Additional resources are needed to implement critical traffic calming and safety projects.  
•
1.0 FTE Pump Station Mechanic - $134,000; Allocated 70% to Sewer Fund and 30% to General Fund
o
The City has a total of 27 sewer and 10 storm pump stations that are currently operated and maintained 
by three Pump Station Mechanics and a Laborer. Ensuring that these critical facilities are properly 
maintained and operated requires additional staff, particularly during wet weather events. Many facilities 
do not have backup power and require the use of portable generators, which are rotated between stations 
to maintain the sewer system from backing up and creating sanitary sewer overflows. The storm pump 
stations require constant clearing of trash to avoid malfunction and flooding. With an additional staff, 
preventive maintenance measures can be more effectively implemented and response times during 
emergencies greatly improved. 
•
1.0 FTE Wastewater Treatment Plant (WWTP) Operator I/II - $139,000; Allocated 100% to Sewer Fund
o
With the expansion of the WWTP, a staff derivation study was conducted to determine the adequate 
number of staff needed to operate the facility. An additional operator is needed to ensure that the facility 
is well monitored and operated to meet regulatory requirements. 
Parks & Recreation Department
The Recreation Division is reorganizing based on current programming needs.  With recent vacancies, the department has 
an opportunity to reallocate resources into more front-line support positions and reduce overhead management positions.  
With this proposed reorganization, the department will eliminate vacancies in the following areas:  the community 
services manager, two supervisor positions, and two lead teacher positions.  
In addition, a section manager position in the Police Activity League (PAL) will be downgraded to a supervisor role. This 
change is due to elimination of funding from PAL for the additional cost of the section manager. 
Those vacancies will allow the addition of four new coordinator positions and one new senior program assistant position 
and the reclassification of a program assistant position to a senior program assistant position.  This structure will allow the 
department to create better workflow, delegation and oversight of work projects and daily tasks, along with creating a 
more streamlined succession plan for long-term outlook and recruitment.  This will allow for better service to the public 
and for streamlining of business operations.  Funding for these changes will be close to budget neutral. These changes are 
subject to bargaining unit approval, which will be in process shortly.

attributable to the General Fund. New additions of $50,000 or more to the operating budget are noted below by 
department:

and evidence processing tables. Funds will also be designated for implementation of a more uniform 
storage system for existing evidence, reducing the chance of lost or damaged items stored for an extended 
period.

Additional cost for digital evidence software - $93,000
o
With the ever-evolving world of technology, upgrades to forensic tools and equipment are necessary. This 
funding will be used to enhance the San Mateo Digital Forensic Extraction Team’s ability to perform full-
file system and physical extractions from computers and cellular phones with the Cellebrite Premium

Service. This new software will assist in accessing highly protected areas such as the iOS Keychain or the 
Secure Folder. Accessing third party application data, stored passwords and tokens, chat conversations, 
location data, email attachments, system logs, as well as deleted content, will increase the unit’s chances 
of finding the incriminating evidence.

PENSION CONTRIBUTIONS
A key component of the cost of employee compensation is the unfunded accrued liability (UAL) with the California Public 
Employees’ Retirement System (CalPERS). The UAL is the amortized cost of CalPERS’ investment gains/losses as well as 
changes in actuarial assumptions; in other words - a loan from CalPERS to the City paid back at 7.0% interest over 30 years 
for investment gains/losses on or before June 30, 2019 and 20 years for investment gains/losses after June 30, 2019. For 
FY 2023-24, the total UAL is $17.6 million; the General Fund’s share of which is $13.5 million. As reflected in the General 
Fund Long-Term Financial Plan, the General Fund’s share of the UAL will precipitously increase to $18.7 million in 2024-25 
and as high as $24.6 million in 2030-31 as a result of CalPERS’ negative 7.5% investment return for the fiscal year ended 
June 30, 2022. Over the course of the 20-year amortization period, CalPERS’ investment loss from June 30, 2022 will 
require the City to pay $177 million more in pension contributions. The escalating cost of pension contributions continues 
to reduce the City’s capacity to address competing demands with its limited resources, including service level 
enhancements and unfunded infrastructure needs.
Transfers Out | Investment in Infrastructure and Capital Improvements
The General Fund supports other needs by transferring resources out to other funds. For fiscal year 2023-24, total 
transfers out are projected to be $14.2 million:
•
$7.25 million to support the City’s capital improvement plan (CIP), which is $3.0 million more than the General 
Fund has historically contributed to the CIP, which is indicative of the City Council’s commitment to continue 
investing in the City’s infrastructure and capital improvements.
•
$3.85 million of Measure S funds to support the CIP;
•
$2.69 million from Measure S to transfer out for debt service related to flood control and street rehabilitation; and
•
$0.41 million to address building maintenance needs
General Fund Long-Term Plan
Over the next 10 years, the General Fund is projected to draw down Rainy Day Reserves to as low as $15 million in 2030-31 
and recover to $25.7 million by 2032-33. Key revenue and expenditure assumptions are noted below:
GENERAL FUND REVENUES:
•
The State of California will continue to honor the escalating VLF shortfall claims. As the number of non-basic aid 
429 of 763

--- page 430 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
school districts continues to diminish, the VLF shortfall will continue to grow. If every school district reaches basic 
aid status in 10 years, the county-wide VLF shortfall could reach upwards of $370 million by 2032-33, of which the 
City’s share would be over $20 million. At present, the State of California’s 2023-24 May 2023 Revised Budget does 
not include appropriations to address San Mateo County’s $33 million VLF shortfall. If the State no longer honors 
the annual VLF shortfall claim or does not make a legislative amendment to address this issue, the City’s revenue 
loss is projected to be over $100 million through 2032-33.
•
Inflation and the Federal Fund Rate will reach 3.0% by 2025-26. These metrics have a direct nexus to the interest 
rates available for purchasing homes, and by extension, the City’s real property transfer tax. 
•
The majority of the City’s TOT base is from business travel, which is expected to return to the 2018-19 baseline by 
2025-26 based on projections from Tourism Economics and Visit California.   
•
If the projections for General Fund revenues and expenditures come to fruition, the General Fund would realize a 
net loss of $12.6 million for 2023-24. As shown in the General Fund Long-Term Plan, based on revenue and 
expenditure assumptions, the City’s Rainy Day Reserve is projected to reach a low of $16.0 million in 2031 before 
recovering to the target reserve policy goal of $30.0 million by 2033.
•
Policy H1.2 of the City’s Housing Element calls for the City to resume contributing 20% of the property tax that the 
City receives as a taxing entity from the former Redevelopment Agency (RDA), also known as ”boomerang funds” 
to the housing set aside in fiscal year 2023-24, which the City can use for affordable housing developments. The 
City suspended its practice due to the immediate and severe financial impact of COVID-19 in 2020. For 2023-24, 
this would result in $1.0 million shifting from the Rainy Day Reserve to the housing set aside. 
GENERAL FUND EXPENDITURES
•
Employee cost of living adjustments (COLA) will keep pace with long-term inflation at 2%.
•
Pension contributions will increase sharply starting in 2024-25 due to CalPERS’ investment losses from 2021-22. 
Over the 20-year amortization period starting in 2024-25, the City will pay $177 million more in pension 
contributions. The General Fund Long-Term Plan assumes that CalPERS will meet its target rate of return (discount 
rate) for the remainder of the 10-year forecast. 
NET REDUCTIONS
Net Reductions reflect potential favorable deviations from revenue or expenditure assumptions. For example, the City’s 
long-term assumption for the increase in AV for property tax is 4%. In any given year, if the AV is greater than the long-
term assumption, the City would recognize additional revenue. Conversely, the City’s vacancy assumption for non-safety 
positions is 2%. If the actual vacancy rate is greater in a fiscal year, then the expenditures would be less than projected. 
The General Fund Long-Term Plan assumes net reductions of $3.1 million to $3.7 million each year.
The chart below illustrates the budget to actual deviations that have come to fruition over the past five fiscal years:

Transportation Improvement Fee and The General Plan
The City’s Municipal Code Chapter 27.13 established a transportation improvement fee (TIF) for new development to help 
fund transportation improvements necessary to maintain intersection improvements that will accommodate future 
growth.  The TIF currently funds transportation improvement projects that are necessary to maintain consistency with the 
General Plan Policy (C2.1), which requires that intersections not exceed a vehicle-specific level of service or a certain 
amount of delay. Policy C2.1 is changing with the forthcoming General Plan Update. As such, the list of projects that are 
required to accommodate future growth will change in order to be consistent with the new General Plan policy. Once the 
General Plan is adopted, staff will develop a new list of traffic improvements that aligns with the updated General Plan 
policy, and then update the TIF. Future transportation improvement projects will be programmed into the capital 
improvement plan once this effort is completed.
The narratives below list each project type, the total CIP cost over the five-year period, and major projects within each 
project type for the 2023-28 CIP. The proposed 2023-24 budget book includes more detailed information on the 2023-28 
CIP, including a charter for new funded projects as well as priority unfunded projects.

CONSTRUCTION SERVICES FUND 
The Construction Services Fund accounts for the revenues and expenditures of the building permit activities. Total 2023-24 
revenues are projected to be $8.8 million, total expenditures are projected to be $12.5 million, and transfers out are 
projected to be $5.5 million. If the proposed budget estimates come to fruition, the Construction Services Fund will result 
in a planned draw on fund balance of $9.2 million, bringing the fund balance closer to the reserve policy of 12 months of 
434 of 763

--- page 435 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
operating expenditures. 
SEWER FUND 
The proposed 2023-24 budget projects $86.5 million in revenues, principally comprised of sewer service fees of $80.6 
million. The projected revenues reflect the anticipated annual rate increases of 3% for fiscal years 2023-24 through 2027-
28 as proposed in a separate agenda item to support the capital improvements and maintain ongoing services. Total 
expenses are projected to be $230.1 million, comprised of $28.5 million in operating expenses, $173.6 million in CIP, and 
$28.1 million in debt service. CIP expenses will largely be funded by the low-interest loans from the state and federal 
government and primarily reflects the commitment to complete the Clean Water Program, a comprehensive plan to 
update the wastewater collection system and wastewater treatment plant (WWTP).
 
GANN LIMIT
In addition to the adoption of the 2023-24 budget and capital improvement program, there will be one other action 
requested of Council as part of the overall budget adoption process on June 20th. This action is to establish the City’s 
appropriations limit for 2023-24. The appropriations limit, which was originally established in 1979 by Proposition 4, places 
a maximum limit of the appropriation of tax proceeds that can be made by the State, school districts, and local 
governments in California. California Government Code requires that the City annually adopt an appropriations limit for 
the upcoming fiscal year. The appropriations limit for the City of San Mateo for 2023-24 is $260,445,497, while the 
proceeds of taxes subject to the appropriations limit is $135,537,117. Therefore, the City is well below its appropriations 
limit for fiscal year 2023-24. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
The overall proposed 2023-24 budget is $317.5 million, comprised of $239.1 million for the operating budget and $78.4 
million for the CIP. The General Fund is projected to account for $159.1 million in revenues and $171.7 million in 
expenditures, reflecting an anticipated draw on the Rainy Day Reserve due to front-loading support for the CIP and the 
impact of the VLF shortfall. The Construction Services Fund is expected to have $8.8 million in revenues and $12.5 million 
in expenditures for a planned draw on reserves of $3.7 million. The Sewer Fund is expected to account for $86.5 million in 
revenues and $230.1 million in expenses, largely comprised of CIP for the Clean Water Program that is primarily funded by 
low-interest loans from the state and federal government.
The 2023-28 CIP is projected to be $284.4 million. Each fiscal year, the City Council only appropriates funds for Year One of 
the five-year CIP. The 2023-24 CIP is projected toBudg be $78.4 million.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This proposed 2023-24 budget is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is a government fiscal activity which does not 
involve any commitment to any specific project which may result in a potentially significant physical impact on the 
environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)

Transmittal of Proposed Budget
City Manager's Letter of Transmittal
Honorable Mayor Lee and Members of the City Council:
The City’s long-term fiscal condition is healthy due to prudent fiscal discipline that the City Council exercised beginning in 
the wake of the Great Recession to accumulate resources in preparation for the next economic fluctuation. The landscape 
of the City’s post-COVID economic recovery reflects a diametrically opposed impact of inflation on major tax revenues. 
While inflation largely spurred the relatively rapid recovery of sales tax revenues back to the pre-pandemic baseline within 
two years, it is also, by extension, unfavorably impacting real property transfer tax (RPTT) revenues due to the high cost to 
borrow money.  
In contrast to the rapid recovery of sales tax revenues, transient occupancy tax revenues (TOT) have yet to recover back to 
the pre-pandemic baseline. However, the recovery trend is better than previously projected. TOT revenues are projected to 
end fiscal year 2022-23 at $5.0 million, which is 71% of the pre-pandemic baseline, and largely reflect the recovery trends 
of business travel, which is the primary base for the City’s TOT revenues. 
While sales tax, RPTT, and TOT are susceptible to economic ebb and flow, the challenges with the property tax in-lieu of 
vehicle license fees (VLF) are due to a structural flaw that began with the swap of property tax with a reduction in vehicle 
license fees from 20 years ago as a part of the 2004 budget deal with the State of California. Due to the limited number of 
non-basic aid school districts in San Mateo County, a VLF shortfall has occurred each year since 2020-21 through present. 
The VLF shortfall issue will be discussed in greater detail later in this letter of transmittal. 
The City has prepared a two-year business plan since 1986, in which the focus of year one of the business plan is on the 
operating budget, and the focus of year two of the business plan is on the Capital Improvement Program (CIP). Due to the 
fiscal uncertainty of the pandemic, the City shifted to a one-year budget beginning in fiscal year 2020-21 and returned to 
the two-year business plan in fiscal year 2022-23. Fiscal year 2023-24 represents year two of the business plan, as such, 
the focus is on the CIP.  
2023-24 Budget and Long-Term Financial Plan Overview 
The proposed 2023-24 budget projects General Fund total revenues of $159.1 million and total expenditures of 
$171.7 million. Of note, the projection for total revenues reflects the State of California’s 2023-24 budget not including 
appropriations to address the 2021-22 county-wide VLF shortfall of $33 million. In addition, the proposed budget includes 
substantial support for the CIP to address the City’s infrastructure needs. 
The City’s General Fund is in a strong fiscal position with an estimated $111.9 million in reserves at the end of FY 2022-23, 
which includes $71.5 million in Rainy Day Reserves, which is above and beyond the 25% operating reserve of $37.3 million. 
In contrast to the City’s limited fiscal position during the Great Recession, City Council’s prudent fiscal discipline during the 
period of recovery and growth post-Great Recession positioned the General Fund to be able to draw on the Rainy Day 
Reserves during economic fluctuations. The Rainy Day Reserves are expected to decline from a projected high of $71.5 
million in 2023 to a low of $21.0 million in 2031 and build to the policy goal of $30.0 million in 2033.
Of note, there are two major assumptions in the General Fund Long-Term Financial Plan: 1) that the State of California will 
honor future VLF shortfall claims; and 2) that the California Public Employees’ Retirement System’s (CalPERS) investment 
earnings will meet its discount rate. If CalPERS’ investment earnings unfavorably deviate from the discount rate, amortized 
investment losses will further diminish the City’s long-term fiscal position.

Transmittal of Proposed Budget
The total City operating budget for 2023-24 is $239.1 million and is up 10.2% increase over the adopted 2022-23 budget. 
The cost escalation is primarily due to capitalized interest from the 2021 Notes, which were issued as a part of the Plan of 
Finance for the Clean Water Program in a prior fiscal year. Absent the capitalized interest, the operating budget increased 
by 3.9% due to the cost of employee compensation, reflecting negotiated compensation increases and escalation in the 
cost of healthcare and pension contributions. Employee compensation (Salaries and Wages, Benefits, and 86.7% of the 
operating contribution to San Mateo Consolidated Fire Department) represents over 60% of the total operating budget, 
which reflects the cost to provide core services to the San Mateo community. 
The CIP for 2023-24 totals $78.0 million, which is $10.1 million more than the adopted 2022-23 CIP, and $16.0 million less 
than the 2023-24 CIP estimate from the adopted 2022-23 CIP. The change in CIP for 2023-24 between the prior 5-year CIP 
and the proposed 2023-24 CIP was primarily in the Sanitary Sewer project type, which shifted some planned project costs 
to 2025-26. Nearly half of the proposed 2023-24 CIP budget is for Sewer Fund capital improvements, and the majority 
of which are for the Clean Water Program annual citywide sanitary sewer rehabilitation ($11.7 million) and Wastewater 
Treatment Plant (WWTP) Flow Management Upgrade and Expansion ($11.2 million) project. 
2023-24 Proposed Budget
With the above recovery trends and challenges in context, I am pleased to present the 2023-24 budget. Overall, the 
proposed citywide budget, including both operating and capital, is $317.1 million. 
The City’s proposed budget for 2023-24 continues to provide for core services, Measure S initiatives, and Council priorities, 
while also making a substantial investment in our capital and infrastructure needs. The following table summarizes the 
proposed spending plans for operating and capital expenditures for 2023-24.

Transmittal of Proposed Budget
Challenges 
The primary fiscal challenges that the City is facing are the impact of inflation on RPTT, the county-wide VLF shortfall and 
the State’s intransigence to honor its commitment to pay the VLF that is owed to the county and the cities within San Mateo 
County, and escalating pension contributions due to CalPERS’ investment losses from 2021-22. Each challenge is discussed 
in greater detail below. 
Real Property Transfer Tax 
While inflation has had a favorable impact on sales tax revenues, it has, by extension, conversely dampened real property 
transfer tax revenues. As shown in Charts 1 and 2, the Federal Reserve’s efforts to slow the economy down by raising the 
federal funds rate has increased interest rates, which makes it more expensive to borrow money as reflected in the average 
mortgage interest rate.
Chart 1: Federal Funds Rate since 1954
Chart 2: Average mortgage interest rate since 1971
6
443 of 763

--- page 444 ---
As shown in Charts 3 and 4, the increased cost to borrow money has dramatically slowed the real estate market down, and 
by extension, has reduced the City’s projected real property transfer tax revenues.
Transmittal of Proposed Budget
Chart 3: Local Real Estate Metrics - Single Family Homes from 2016 - 2023
7
444 of 763

--- page 445 ---
Transmittal of Proposed Budget
The reduction in the number of properties sold and the median sales price as well as the increase in the average days on 
market have dampened the City’s real property transfer tax revenues in comparison to prior trends:
While the Federal Open Market Committee anticipates that the federal funds rate will gradually decline over the next couple 
of years, the impact of higher interest rates is the primary impetus for resetting the long-term assumption for RPTT to $8 
million per fiscal year in the General Fund Long-Term Financial Plan. 
Property Tax In-Lieu of Vehicle License Fees  
In 2004, as a part of the budget deal with the State of California, the vehicle license fee rate was reduced from 2.0% to 
0.65%. To offset the onerous financial impact to local governments, the State backfilled the reduction of vehicle license 
fees with property tax, known as property tax in-lieu of vehicle license fees (VLF). The primary caveat being that the funding 
source for the VLF is property tax and Educational Revenue Augmentation Fund (ERAF) from non-basic aid school districts, 
which are school districts that are not able to meet their State-determined minimum funding level (as defined by Proposition 
98) through property taxes. The property tax and ERAF from non-basic aid school districts that is used to fund the VLF owed
to cities and counties is then backfilled by the State General Fund.
In San Mateo County, 17 of the 23 school districts have basic aid status. As such, the limited number of non-basic aid school 
districts to backfill the property tax in-lieu of VLF that is owed to the county and local cities has resulted in a shortfall. 
To date, San Mateo County is the only county in the State of California that has had VLF shortfalls. At present, the only 
recourse to be made whole for the VLF shortfall is to file a claim with the State for it to consider as a part of its budget 
deliberations in the subsequent fiscal year. In fiscal year (FY) 2020-21, the county-wide VLF shortfall was $96 million, and 
the City’s share of the VLF shortfall was $5.7 million. The State’s 2022-23 adopted budget included appropriations to make 
San Mateo County whole for the 2020-21 VLF shortfall. However, the State’s proposed 2023-24 budget that was published 
in January 2023 did not include any budget appropriations for the FY 2021-22 VLF shortfall of $33 million. Despite the 
collective efforts from San Mateo County and every local city within San Mateo County to contact state representatives to 
Chart 4: Five-Year Historical Real Property Transfer Tax by Quarter
8
445 of 763

--- page 446 ---
Transmittal of Proposed Budget
address the 2021-22 VLF shortfall, the appropriation was not included in the State's May 2023 revise budget. Over the next 
7-10 years, it is expected that the remaining non-basic aid school districts will gradually shift to basic aid status, and as such,
the VLF shortfall will grow exponentially, from the shortfall of $70 million for FY 2022-23 to over $370 million by 2032-33,
of which the City’s share would be approximately $20 million. The General Fund Long-Term Plan assumes that the State will
honor San Mateo County’s VLF shortfall claim each year.
Pension  
In July 2021, CalPERS reported a preliminary return on investment (ROI ) of 21.3% for fiscal year 2020-21. At first blush, 
higher interest returns may have seemed favorable, but interest is a measure of risk, and the deviation from the discount 
rate meant that CalPERS’ investment portfolio was taking on too much risk. As such, the Funding Risk Mitigation Policy 
(RMP) was triggered, which reduced the discount rate from 7.0% to 6.8%. The investment gain – the difference between 
the 7.0% discount rate and the 21.3% ROI, will be amortized over 20 years, with a ramp up over the first five years (1/5th or 
20% of the annual amortization amount recognized each year), beginning in the actuarial valuations with a measurement 
date of June 30, 2021, which applies to the pension contribution rates for 2023-24. The reduction of the discount rate will 
be amortized over 20 years with no ramping, as ramping for changes in actuarial assumptions was eliminated beginning in 
measurement periods of June 30, 2019. 
Due to the high degree of market volatility, in the fiscal year ended June 30, 2022, CalPERS reported an investment return 
of negative 7.5%. Of note, the investment loss that will be amortized over a 20 year period beginning in FY 2024-25 is 
the difference between negative 7.5% and the discount rate of 6.8%. Once the five-year ramping has been completed, 
the increase in pension contributions as a result of the loss will be an additional $10 million per year. Over the 20-year 
amortization period, the City will pay $177 million in additional pension contributions as a result of CalPERS’ investment loss 
from FY 2021-22.
Chart 5: CalPERS ROI and discount rate 1992 - 2022
9
446 of 763

--- page 447 ---
Transmittal of Proposed Budget
Every deviation between the ROI and the discount rate (gain or loss) results in an amortization base being generated, 
which is essentially a mortgage or loan that is paid off over time. Gains or losses incurred before June 30, 2019 have an 
amortization period of 30 years, while subsequent gains or losses are amortized over 20 years; a policy change to reduce 
the cost of interest expense. In addition to the payment on the UAL, the City’s pension contribution also includes the normal 
cost, which is the cost for the current year of service. Assuming that CalPERS will be able to achieve its long-term ROI of 
6.8%, as shown in the table below, it takes nearly all of the City’s sales tax revenues, which is second to only property tax as 
the General Fund’s top tax revenue source, to cover the escalating cost of pension contributions for the General Fund.
